teachings.teaching: Various materials (clay, herbs, grains, metals, gems) are collected for the ritual, each with symbolic significance.
teachings.lens_note: The gathering of diverse substances symbolizes the integration of all elements of creation into the worship, a key theme in Kriya-Yoga where the microcosm reflects the macrocosm.

teachings.teaching: One should not be miserly (vittaśāṭhya) in performing the ritual; generosity is essential.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that non-attachment to wealth is a form of inner purification, necessary for the ritual to bear fruit.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Adhivāsanavidhiḥ' (Ritual of Consecration), provides a detailed guide for the consecration of a Śiva liṅga. It covers purification rites, installation of deities and guardians, nyāsa (placement of mantras and energies), homa (fire offerings), and the final establishment of the liṅga. The text emphasizes the integration of tattvas (principles) and the role of the guru in ensuring the ritual's efficacy.

story.title: Adhivāsanavidhiḥ: The Ritual of Consecration
story.arc: Ritual manual for installing a Śiva liṅga, covering purification, invocation, nyāsa, homa, and final establishment.
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes a ritual sequence without a narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical ritual manual with no dramatic narrative.

teachings.teaching: The consecration ritual (adhivāsana) is essential for making the liṅga a fit abode for Śiva; without it, worship bears no fruit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores that external ritual must be internalized as a yogic process of purification and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The guru, through proper nyāsa and homa, infuses the liṅga with the 36 tattvas, making it a microcosm of the universe.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as aligning the practitioner's subtle body with cosmic principles, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The practitioner should visualize the descent of Śiva's energy through the brahmarandhra and into the liṅga, uniting the individual self with the supreme.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga technique of raising kundalini and merging with Śiva at the crown chakra.

teachings.teaching: The homa (fire offering) with specific mantras and materials purifies the environment and invokes the deities, ensuring the ritual's success.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret homa as the external expression of inner tapas (austerity) and surrender.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 97) provides a detailed ritual manual for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of a Śiva liṅga. It covers preliminary rites, placement of the liṅga, installation procedures, homa (fire offerings), and concluding ceremonies, including the establishment of attendant deities and the handling of various types of liṅgas.

story.title: Chapter 97
story.arc: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 97) provides a detailed ritual manual for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of a Śiva liṅga. It covers preliminary rites, placement of the liṅga, installation procedures, homa (fire offerings), and concluding ceremonies, including the establishment of attendant deities and the handling of various types of liṅgas.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for establishing (pratiṣṭhā) the goddess Gaurī, including the construction of the maṇḍapa, installation of the image, placement of various śaktis (powers), and the specific mantras and offerings to be used.

story.title: Gaurīpratiṣṭhākathana
story.arc: Ritual installation of Goddess Gaurī
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; purely ritual instruction.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical manual for ritual.

teachings.teaching: The installation of Gaurī involves the placement of various śaktis (powers) such as Kriyāśakti, Jñānaśakti, and Vyāpinīśakti, representing the dynamic, cognitive, and pervasive aspects of the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the three śaktis correspond to the stages of action (kriyā), knowledge (jñāna), and expansion (vyāpinī), which are essential for spiritual practice. The ritual externalizes inner yogic processes.

teachings.teaching: The mantra 'oṃ hrīṃ saḥ mahāgauri rudradayite svāhā' is used for worship, linking Gaurī to Rudra (Śiva) as his consort.
teachings.lens_note: This mantra embodies the union of Śiva and Śakti, a core principle in Kriya-Yoga where the practitioner seeks to unite individual consciousness with universal consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for establishing (pratishtha) an image of the Sun god (Surya). It details the preparatory rituals, the construction of a mandapa, the placement of the deity, the installation of mantras, and the purification processes.

story.title: Establishment of the Sun God
story.arc: Ritual procedure for installing an image of Surya.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; it is a prescriptive ritual text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical ritual manual.

teachings.teaching: The installation of Surya involves a mandapa, bathing, and placing the deity on a seat or bed, along with the installation of mantras and the five elements.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this ritual symbolizes the internal establishment of the sun (consciousness) in the body through disciplined action (kriya), where the mandapa represents the body, the deity represents the self, and the mantras represent the vibrational energies that purify and align the practitioner.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for establishing a door (dvārapratiṣṭhā), including the purification of door components, placement of mantras, offerings, and homa.

story.title: Door Installation Ritual
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; purely ritual instruction.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical ritual description.

teachings.teaching: The door installation involves purifying the door parts with astringents and placing them on a bed, then arranging the three principles (Ātman, etc.) in the root, middle, and top sections.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the alignment of the practitioner's body (door) with the inner self (Ātman) through purificatory actions.

teachings.teaching: After placing the principles and performing homa and japa, one should worship the Vāstu deity and deposit the five gems, followed by a peace offering.
teachings.lens_note: The five gems represent the five elements; their placement grounds the ritual in material reality, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's integration of physical and spiritual.

teachings.teaching: A bundle of auspicious substances (barley, white mustard, etc.) tied with udumbara wood is placed under the door for protection, using the praṇava mantra.
teachings.lens_note: The praṇava (Om) is the foundational vibration; this act seals the door with cosmic sound, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on mantra as energy.

teachings.teaching: The door should be installed slightly to the north, with the Ātman principle below and the Vidyātattva at the sides, while Śiva is placed in the space and Maheśanātha with the root mantra.
teachings.lens_note: This spatial arrangement mirrors the yogic body: Ātman at the base (mūlādhāra), Vidyātattva as channels (nāḍīs), and Śiva as consciousness (sahasrāra).

teachings.teaching: Offerings of oblations (homa) should be made with names of the door components, one hundred or half that number, or double according to capacity, to remove defects.
teachings.lens_note: The number of oblations corresponds to the practitioner's energy level; Kriya-Yoga adapts practices to individual capacity, emphasizing inner readiness over external quantity.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for consecrating a temple (prāsādapratiṣṭhā). It details the placement of a pot (kumbha) with sacred substances, the infusion of life force (prāṇa) into the image through breath control and mantras, and the installation of various deities and energies. The process involves meditation on Rudra and Umā, and culminates in offerings and homa.

story.title: Temple Consecration (Prāsādapratiṣṭhā)
story.arc: Ritual instruction
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; prescriptive ritual text.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical ritual description.

teachings.teaching: The process of infusing life (prāṇa) into an image through breath control (prāṇāyāma) and mantras, connecting the microcosm (body) with the macrocosm (deity).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the internal practice of prāṇāyāma to awaken the dormant energy (kuṇḍalinī) and unite the individual self with the cosmic consciousness. The external ritual mirrors the internal yogic process.

teachings.teaching: The installation of the deity's presence (sānnidhya) through homa, sprinkling, touch, and japa, divided into three parts.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the threefold purification of body, speech, and mind necessary for divine communion. The external acts correspond to internal disciplines: homa as surrender, sprinkling as purification, touch as prāṇa transmission, japa as mental focus.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of hoisting a flag (dhvajāropaṇa) on a temple or shrine. It details the construction of the flagpole, the materials, the mantras, the placement of various deities and tattvas, and the benefits for the patron. The procedure involves purification, meditation, offerings, and homa to ensure auspiciousness.

story.title: The Ritual of Hoisting the Flag
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Mythological time; no specific historical context.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical ritual manual.

teachings.teaching: The flagpole should be made of bamboo or sal wood, and its dimensions are prescribed according to the quality (uttama, madhyama, adhama).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the flagpole represents the spine (merudanda), and the flag is the kundalini energy. The dimensions correspond to the three gunas.

teachings.teaching: If the flagpole breaks during installation, it indicates misfortune for the king or patron; a pacification ritual should be performed.
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes a disruption in the flow of prana; the shanti ritual restores balance.

teachings.teaching: The flagpole is infused with various tattvas (principles) and deities: at the base, the earth tattva; then water, fire, air, ether; and at the top, Shiva tattva.
teachings.lens_note: This maps to the chakra system: muladhara to sahasrara, with the flag representing the union of individual consciousness with Shiva.

teachings.teaching: The patron, along with friends and relatives, should circumambulate the temple after the flag is hoisted, obtaining the desired fruit.
teachings.lens_note: Circumambulation (pradakshina) is a form of kriya-yoga, aligning the microcosm with the macrocosm.

teachings.teaching: The guru should meditate on the Pashupata form of Shiva, surrounded by mantra-adhipas, and instruct the guardian deities to protect the temple.
teachings.lens_note: This meditation stabilizes the prana and invokes the protective aspect of Shiva, ensuring the success of the ritual.

teachings.teaching: After performing homa and offering bali to the directions to counteract defects, the guru should be given dakshina, and the patron attains heaven.
teachings.lens_note: The homa purifies the elements; dakshina is an offering of gratitude, completing the cycle of karma.

teachings.teaching: The number of atoms in the image, linga, and vedi determines the number of thousands of years the patron enjoys the fruit.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the infinite merit of properly consecrated structures, linking material construction to spiritual reward.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for renovating or replacing a damaged or old Shiva linga (jīrṇoddhāra). It details the rituals, mantras, and offerings required to properly remove the old linga and install a new one, emphasizing the importance of following the prescribed rites to avoid negative consequences.

story.title: Procedure for Renovation of a Damaged Shiva Linga
story.arc: Ritual instruction
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: A damaged or old Shiva linga should not be moved casually; proper rituals must be followed for its removal and replacement.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this emphasizes the sanctity of physical objects as supports for meditation and the need for disciplined action (kriya) in maintaining sacred spaces.

teachings.teaching: The guru should perform homa (fire sacrifice) with specific offerings to purify and invoke blessings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a form of kriya-yoga where external rituals align internal energies, purifying the mind and environment for higher practice.

teachings.teaching: The old linga should be respectfully removed and immersed in water, while a new one is installed with mantras.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of letting go of the old (past karma) and embracing the new (fresh spiritual resolve) through conscious ritual action.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the general characteristics of temple architecture, including the layout of the sanctum, pillars, doors, and various types of temples classified by shape and style. It provides measurements and proportions for different parts of the temple, and warns against certain defects in construction.

story.title: Temple Architecture
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of Agni Purana's architectural section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical description.

teachings.teaching: The temple should be built with precise proportions; the sanctum (garbha) should be one part, the pillar (piṇḍikā) two parts, etc.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, precise proportions symbolize the inner alignment of body, mind, and spirit; the temple is a microcosm of the yogic body.

teachings.teaching: Doors should be placed in the cardinal directions, not in the intermediate ones.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the importance of directional alignment in meditation and ritual, focusing energy along the main axis.

teachings.teaching: Defects in construction (e.g., piercing a pillar, tree, well, or field) bring misfortune such as servitude, fear, loss of wealth, or bondage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as metaphors for internal obstacles: piercing the pillar (steadiness) leads to instability; piercing the well (source of life) leads to fear.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 105) provides detailed instructions on the layout and construction of towns, villages, forts, and houses according to Vastu principles. It describes the 81-pada (81-square) grid for worship, the placement of deities in various directions, and the dimensions and features of different types of buildings, including one, two, three, four, and eight-halled houses. The chapter also discusses the auspicious and inauspicious effects of various structural features and the placement of rooms for specific purposes.

story.title: Nagaradi Vastu Kathana
story.arc: Instructional discourse on town and house planning
story.timeline_note: Part of the Agni Purana, likely a dialogue between Ishvara and Guha (Skanda).
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily technical instructions.

teachings.teaching: The layout of towns and houses should follow the 81-pada grid, with deities worshipped in specific positions to ensure success.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning one's environment with cosmic order (Rta) to support spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The dimensions of buildings should be based on specific proportions (e.g., length 27 hasta, width 28 hasta) to avoid defects.
teachings.lens_note: Proportional harmony in construction mirrors the inner harmony sought through Kriya-Yoga, where the body is a temple.

teachings.teaching: Different types of houses (one, two, three, four, eight-halled) have specific auspicious and inauspicious effects depending on their layout and orientation.
teachings.lens_note: The choice of house type corresponds to the practitioner's stage of spiritual development; a balanced layout supports balanced sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Rooms should be placed according to directional deities: kitchen in Agni, bedroom in Yama, treasury in Kubera, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns the microcosm of the house with the macrocosm, facilitating the flow of prana and aiding meditation.

teachings.teaching: The central part of the house should be open (courtyard) and the mandapa should be in the middle for auspiciousness.
teachings.lens_note: The open center represents the heart space (hridaya) in Kriya-Yoga, where the divine is realized.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 106) provides detailed instructions on the construction of cities, towns, forts, and houses (nagarādivāstu). It covers the layout, placement of gates, allocation of quarters for different castes and professions, orientation of buildings, and the importance of dedicating structures to deities to avoid affliction by evil spirits. The chapter emphasizes that properly designed settlements bring victory, enjoyment, and liberation.

story.title: Instructions on City and House Construction
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Agni Purana's architectural section.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual with no narrative events.

teachings.teaching: Cities and houses should be built with proper orientation and placement of gates, and dedicated to deities to avoid affliction by evil spirits.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching emphasizes that external structures must align with inner spiritual principles. The proper layout of a city or house mirrors the alignment of one's inner energies (kriyas) for harmonious living and spiritual progress. Dedicating structures to deities symbolizes surrendering the ego and inviting divine grace, which purifies the environment and supports meditation.

teachings.teaching: Different castes and professions should be assigned specific directions within the settlement.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as a metaphor for the organization of one's inner faculties. Each direction corresponds to a specific energy center (chakra) or function. Placing the right activities in the right directions ensures that one's actions (kriyas) are in harmony with cosmic order, leading to efficiency and spiritual upliftment.

teachings.teaching: A settlement without a presiding deity is afflicted by evil spirits and diseases.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that without a connection to the divine (the inner Self), one's life and environment become chaotic and prone to negative influences. Establishing a deity (or a sacred space) is akin to anchoring one's practice in the divine, which protects and purifies the mind and body.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the creation of the world according to the Svayambhuva Manvantara, detailing the distribution of the seven continents (dvīpas) by Priyavrata to his sons, the geography of Jambudvīpa with its eight subcontinents (varṣas), and the lineage of kings from Ṛṣabha to Satyajit. It also mentions the blissful nature of the subcontinents and the yogic attainment of Ṛṣabha.

story.title: Svāyambhuvasarga: The Creation of the World
story.arc: Cosmogony and genealogy from Priyavrata to Satyajit
story.timeline_note: Svāyambhuva Manvantara; events span from Priyavrata's distribution of continents to the lineage of kings ending with Satyajit.
story.comic_potential: The orderly distribution of continents and the long list of royal genealogies could be visualized as a cosmic map and a dynastic chart.

teachings.teaching: The eight subcontinents (varṣas) like Kimpuruṣa are naturally blissful, free from old age, death, fear, and the duality of dharma and adharma; they are attained effortlessly.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this describes the state of sahaja samadhi, where the practitioner abides in natural bliss without effort, transcending the dualities of karma and time.

teachings.teaching: Ṛṣabha, after giving his kingdom to his sons, performed penance at Śālagrāma and attained absorption into Viṣṇu.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga path of renunciation and focused meditation (tapas) leading to union with the divine, emphasizing that liberation is achieved through inner discipline.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the geography of the seven continents (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, focusing on Jambūdvīpa and Mount Meru. It details the mountains, rivers, forests, and regions within Jambūdvīpa, including the nine varṣas (regions) and the locations of various celestial beings and lokapālas.

story.title: Cosmography of the Seven Continents
story.arc: Geographical description of the universe
story.timeline_note: Timeless; describes the eternal structure of the world.
story.comic_potential: Visualizing the concentric continents and oceans with Mount Meru at the center could be depicted as a cosmic map.

teachings.teaching: The world is structured as a lotus with Mount Meru as the pericarp and the continents as petals.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the lotus of the heart (hṛdaya padma) where the yogi visualizes the cosmos within.

teachings.teaching: The regions beyond the boundary mountains are free from sin and are abodes of gods.
teachings.lens_note: This corresponds to the state of purity achieved through Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner transcends worldly impurities.

chapter_summary: Agni describes the glory of sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites), emphasizing the importance of self-control, fasting, charity, and visiting tirthas. He lists numerous tirthas across India, including Pushkara, Kurukshetra, Varanasi, Prayaga, and many others, and declares that bathing, giving donations, or even residing there grants liberation and the fruits of sacrifices.

story.title: Glory of Tirthas
story.arc: Agni describes the merits of pilgrimage sites and the practices to attain liberation.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of sacred places and their benefits.

teachings.teaching: One who has controlled hands, feet, and mind, and possesses learning, austerity, and fame, attains the fruit of pilgrimage.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes inner purification (yamas and niyamas) as the foundation for external practices like pilgrimage.

teachings.teaching: A pilgrim who abstains from accepting gifts, eats lightly, controls senses, and is free from sin obtains the fruit of all sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on tapas (austerity) and sense control as essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Without fasting for three nights and visiting tirthas, and without giving gold and cows, one becomes poor (in merit).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret 'poor' as spiritual poverty; the practices are means to accumulate punya (merit) through action.

teachings.teaching: The fruit obtained by pilgrimage equals that of a sacrifice.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the principle that sincere external acts, when combined with inner discipline, yield the same result as elaborate rituals.

teachings.teaching: Pushkara is the supreme tirtha; Brahma resides there with gods and sages. Bathing there and worshipping ancestors and gods grants the fruit of Ashvamedha and leads to Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Pushkara represents a place of intense spiritual energy; in Kriya-Yoga, such places amplify the effects of sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Giving food on Karttika full moon leads to purity and Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Timing (Karttika) is considered spiritually potent; charity done at such times yields greater results.

teachings.teaching: Residing, performing japa, and offering shraddha at Pushkara uplifts a hundred generations.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of place and intention; collective karma can be purified through such acts.

teachings.teaching: One who constantly says 'I will go to Kurukshetra, I reside in Kurukshetra' becomes pure and attains heaven.
teachings.lens_note: The power of sankalpa (resolve) and constant remembrance is emphasized; in Kriya-Yoga, this is a form of mental pilgrimage.

teachings.teaching: At Kurukshetra, even the dust leads to the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: The sacredness of the place is so great that even physical contact with its elements confers spiritual benefit.

teachings.teaching: Bathing in Sarasvati at Sannihiti leads to Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Specific tirthas have specific benefits; the confluence of rivers is considered especially powerful.

chapter_summary: Agni describes the glory of the Ganga, stating that she grants both enjoyment and liberation, and that the lands through which she flows become pure and excellent.

story.title: The Glory of the Ganga
story.arc: Agni narrates the greatness of the Ganga river.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a larger discourse on tirthas.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic description.

teachings.teaching: The Ganga is to be served as she grants both enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the Ganga's grace supports both worldly life and spiritual liberation, aligning with the practice of surrendering to divine energy.

teachings.teaching: The lands through which the Ganga flows become pure and excellent.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the purifying power of sacred rivers, which in Kriya-Yoga can be seen as a metaphor for the flow of prana that purifies the nadis.

teachings.teaching: The Ganga is the ultimate refuge for beings seeking progress.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the importance of surrendering to the divine current (kriya) as the means to spiritual advancement.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the greatness of Prayaga (the confluence of Ganga and Yamuna) as a sacred place. It explains that bathing, giving charity, performing shraddha, and dying there grant liberation and heavenly rewards. Various tirthas within Prayaga are enumerated, and the chapter emphasizes the supreme merit of Prayaga over other holy sites.

story.title: The Greatness of Prayaga
story.arc: Description of the sacredness and benefits of Prayaga
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless description of the tirtha's glory.
story.comic_potential: Visual depiction of the confluence, divine beings, and the various tirthas could be striking.

teachings.teaching: Prayaga is the supreme tirtha where Brahma, Vishnu, and other deities reside. Bathing, charity, and shraddha there yield inexhaustible merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of sacred geography as a support for inner purification and the accumulation of spiritual merit through action (kriya).

teachings.teaching: Dying at Prayaga grants liberation and entry to Vishnu's abode.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga focus on the moment of death as a critical opportunity for liberation, where conscious awareness and devotion can lead to moksha.

teachings.teaching: The mere touch of the mud from Prayaga or chanting its name removes all sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the power of sankalpa (intention) and the transformative effect of sacred contact, which purifies the subtle body.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the glory of Varanasi (Kashi) as narrated by Lord Shiva to Goddess Gauri. It highlights Varanasi as the supreme holy place, bestowing both worldly enjoyment and liberation. The chapter lists eight secret sacred sites within Avimukta (Varanasi), explains the etymology of Varanasi as the land between the rivers Varana and Asi, and declares that all religious acts performed there yield liberation.

story.title: The Glory of Varanasi
story.arc: Shiva describes the supreme holy place Varanasi to Gauri, listing its secret sites and the benefits of residing there.
story.timeline_note: Mythological time; no specific historical context.
story.comic_potential: A divine geography lesson with Shiva as the tour guide, revealing hidden sacred spots.

teachings.teaching: Varanasi is the supreme tirtha, bestowing both enjoyment and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the sacred place is not merely external but represents the inner state of awareness where all actions become liberating.

teachings.teaching: Avimukta (Varanasi) is never abandoned; it is the eternal abode of Shiva.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret 'never abandoned' as the constant presence of consciousness; the seeker should never leave the inner awareness of the Self.

teachings.teaching: All acts in Varanasi—bathing, chanting, sacrifice, death, worship, shraddha, charity, residence—yield liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this means that when performed with inner awareness, every action becomes a means to liberation, not just ritual.

chapter_summary: Agni describes the glory of the Narmada river and the sacred site of Amarakaṇṭaka, including the confluence with the Kāverī, the Śrīparvata mountain, and the benefits of pilgrimage, charity, and penance there.

story.title: Glory of Narmada and Amarakaṇṭaka
story.arc: Agni narrates the sacred geography and benefits of the Narmada region, including the story of Gaurī's penance and the presence of Hara and Hiraṇyakaśipu.
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; no specific chronology.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Hara sporting with Devyā and Hiraṇyakaśipu's penance could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Narmada river purifies instantly upon sight, and the waters of the Narmada are more purifying than the Ganges.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the immediate transformative power of sacred contact, akin to the direct experience of prana.

teachings.teaching: Pilgrimage, charity, penance, and śrāddha performed at Amarakaṇṭaka yield inexhaustible merit.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the importance of sankalpa (intention) and the amplification of spiritual practice at power spots.

teachings.teaching: Death at this tīrtha leads to Śivaloka, the highest goal.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's focus on conscious exit and merging with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the glory of Gayā-tīrtha. It describes how the demon Gayāsura performed severe penance, causing distress to the gods. Viṣṇu granted him a boon that he would become the holiest of all tīrthas. Later, Brahmā requested Gayāsura's body for a sacrifice, and the demon agreed. During the sacrifice, Gayāsura began to move, so Viṣṇu placed a divine stone (śilā) on him to keep him steady. The stone became the sacred Gayā-śilā, where Viṣṇu as Gadādhara resides. The chapter also tells the story of Dharmavratā, who was cursed to become a stone but later blessed to be the divine stone. Finally, it describes the establishment of Gayā-kṣetra and the benefits of performing rites there.

story.title: The Glory of Gayā-tīrtha
story.arc: Establishment of Gayā as a sacred pilgrimage site
story.timeline_note: The story spans from the time of Gayāsura's penance to the Pāṇḍavas' worship at Gayā, likely in different yugas.
story.comic_potential: High; features a demon gaining a boon, a moving body held down by a stone, a curse and blessing cycle, and divine weapons.

teachings.teaching: The power of penance and boons: Gayāsura's tapas made him invincible, but Viṣṇu's boon turned him into a holy site.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, tapas is the disciplined practice that transforms the practitioner; the boon represents the fruit of that practice, which when dedicated to the divine, becomes a source of liberation for others.

teachings.teaching: The importance of steadfastness: The stone (śilā) placed on Gayāsura symbolizes stability, and Viṣṇu's presence ensures the tīrtha's permanence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the mind must be made steady like a stone through Kriya-Yoga; the divine presence (Gadādhara) is the anchor that prevents the mind from wandering.

teachings.teaching: The efficacy of rites at Gayā: Performing śrāddha and offerings at Gayā leads to liberation of ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views such rites as external aids that complement internal practice; the true 'Gayā' is the state of awareness where one offers the ego to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The curse and blessing of the brāhmaṇas: Those who accepted gifts at the sacrifice were cursed to be greedy but later blessed to be supported by the tīrtha.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the law of karma: attachment leads to bondage, but when redirected to service (tīrtha), it becomes a means of grace.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the pilgrimage to Gayā, detailing the rituals, tīrthas (sacred sites), and the benefits of performing śrāddha and piṇḍadāna there. It emphasizes that a son's pilgrimage to Gayā liberates ancestors from hell and grants them heaven or mokṣa. The chapter includes instructions for bathing, offerings, and mantras, along with a story of King Viśāla who attained liberation through piṇḍadāna.

story.title: The Pilgrimage to Gayā
story.arc: Description of the Gayāyātrā (pilgrimage to Gayā) with rituals, tīrthas, and the story of King Viśāla's liberation through piṇḍadāna.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a prescriptive text describing the pilgrimage; the story of Viśāla is illustrative.
story.comic_potential: The vision of ancestors in different colors (white, red, black) and their liberation could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: A son's pilgrimage to Gayā liberates ancestors from hell and grants them heaven or mokṣa.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of intentional action (kriya) to transform the subtle body of ancestors, aligning with the principle of karma and grace.

teachings.teaching: Bathing in the tīrthas of Gayā, especially Uttaramānasa and Dakṣiṇamānasa, purifies and grants liberation.
teachings.lens_note: The two manasas (mental lakes) symbolize the purification of the mind (manas) through disciplined practice, a core Kriya-Yoga concept.

teachings.teaching: Piṇḍadāna (offering of rice balls) at specific padas (footprints) like Rudrapada, Viṣṇupada, and Brahmapada liberates ancestors and oneself.
teachings.lens_note: Each pada corresponds to a level of consciousness; offering at these points aligns the practitioner with higher states, reflecting the yogic ascent.

teachings.teaching: The story of King Viśāla demonstrates that piṇḍadāna at Gayāśira can liberate even those in hell and grant progeny.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the law of karma and the efficacy of ritual action (kriya) when performed with faith, a key tenet of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Gayā pilgrimage (Gayāyātrā) rituals, including bathing, sandhyā worship, and offering piṇḍa (rice balls) at various sacred sites in Gayā. It lists numerous tīrthas (holy spots) and deities to be worshipped, emphasizing the benefits of performing these rites for the liberation of ancestors and oneself from the three debts. The chapter concludes with a hymn to Gadādhara (Viṣṇu) and a statement on the merit of reciting the Gayā Māhātmya.

story.title: Gayāyātrāvidhi: The Procedure of the Gayā Pilgrimage
story.arc: Ritual instructions for performing the Gayā pilgrimage, including bathing, sandhyā, piṇḍadāna at various tīrthas, worship of deities, and a hymn to Gadādhara.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter presents a timeless ritual guide.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a procedural text with lists of tīrthas and deities.

teachings.teaching: One should bathe in the Mahānadī with the Gāyatrī mantra and perform sandhyā.
Offer piṇḍa at the front of Gāyatrī in the morning, and at noon bathe with music and worship, then perform sandhyā before Sāvitrī and offer piṇḍa at her place.
Enter the Yonidvāra at Agastya's place; having exited, one does not enter a womb again and is freed from existence.
Offer bali at Kākaśilā and bow to Kumāra; at Svargadvārī, Somakuṇḍa, Vāyutīrtha, offer piṇḍa.
Offer piṇḍa at Ākāśagaṅgā and Kapilā; bow to Kapileśa (Śiva) and offer piṇḍa at Rukmikuṇḍa.
At Koṭītīrtha, bow to Koṭīśa; at Amoghapada, at Gadālola, Vānaraka, Gopracāra, offer piṇḍa.
Bow to the cow at Vaitaraṇī, one uplifts twenty-one generations; the piṇḍa-giver at Krauñcapada becomes so.
Offer piṇḍa at Tṛtīyā, Viśālā, Niścirā; at Ṛṇamokṣa, Pāpamokṣa, bathe with ash at Bhasmakuṇḍa, freed from sin, bow to Janārdana.
Recite: 'This piṇḍa given by me into your hand, Janārdana, may it become imperishable for me who has gone to the other world.'
Janārdana himself in Gayā in the form of the ancestors; seeing Puṇḍarīkākṣa, one is freed from the three debts.
Bow to Mārkaṇḍeyeśvara and Gṛdhreśvara; at Mūlakṣetra of Maheśa, at Dhārā, offer piṇḍa.
At Gṛdhrakūṭa, Gṛdhravaṭa, Dhautapāda, offer piṇḍa; at Puṣkariṇī, Kardamāla, Rāmatīrtha, offer piṇḍa; bow to Prabhāseśa, at Pretaśilā offer piṇḍa.
The ancestors, relatives, etc., stationed in heaven, atmosphere, and earth, and those in preta form, are freed by the piṇḍas given by me.
Pretaśilā at Gayāśiras is purifying; at Prabhāsa Pretakuṇḍa, the piṇḍa-giver uplifts the family.
Bow to Vasiṣṭheśa and offer piṇḍa before him; at Gayānābhi, Suṣumṇā, Mahākoṣṭhī, offer piṇḍa.
Before Gadādhara, at Muṇḍapṛṣṭha, in the presence of Devī, bow to Muṇḍapṛṣṭha first along with Kṣetrapāla etc.; worshipping removes fear and destroys poison, disease, etc.
Bow to Brahmā and lead the family to Brahmaloka; worship Subhadrā, Balabhadra, and Puruṣottama; endowed with all desires, uplift the family and enjoy heaven.
Bow to Hṛṣīkeśa and offer piṇḍa before him; worship Mādhava and become a vaimānika (one who rides a celestial chariot).
Worship Mahālakṣmī, Gaurī, Maṅgalā, and Sarasvatī.
Worship the twelve Ādityas, Vahni, Revanta, Indra; freed from disease etc., one becomes a svargī; worship Śrīkapardī and Vināyaka.
Worship Kārttikeya and become unhindered, attain success; worship Somanātha, Kāleśa, Kedāra, Prapitāmaha, Siddheśvara, Rudreśa, Rāmeśa, Brahmakeśvara — the eight secret liṅgas — and obtain all.
Bow to Nārāyaṇa, Varāha, Nārasiṃha for prosperity; worship Brahmaviṣṇumaheśa, Tripuraghna, the giver of all.
Worship Sītā, Rāma, Garuḍa, Vāmana; obtain all desires and lead the ancestors to Brahmaloka.
Worship the primeval Gadādhara along with the gods; freed from the three debts, uplift the entire family.
The stone is divine and holy; therefore the stone is full of divinity. In Gayā, there is no place where a tīrtha does not exist.
By whose name one drops a piṇḍa, that leads to eternal Brahman. Bow to Phalgvīśa, Phalgucaṇḍī, and Aṅgārakeśvara.
At Mataṅga's place, one should perform śrāddha; at Bharatāśrama, at Haṃsatīrtha, Koṭitīrtha, one should offer piṇḍa and liberate all ancestors.
Perform sandhyā in the evening and bow to Sarasvatī; one who performs trisandhyā becomes a brāhmaṇa proficient in Vedas and Vedāṅgas.
Circumambulate Gayā and worship the brāhmaṇas of Gayā; all gifts of food etc. become imperishable there.

Praise and pray to the primeval deity Gadādhara: 'I bow to Gadādhara, resident of Gayā, giver of destination to ancestors etc., giver of yoga for dharma, artha, kāma, mokṣa; devoid of body, senses, mind, intellect, prāṇa, ego; eternally pure, endowed with intellect, truth, Brahman; blissful, non-dual, worshipped by gods and demons; accompanied by hosts of gods and goddesses; destroyer of the afflictions of Kali; wearing a garland of forest flowers; protector of all worlds; intent on uplifting families; situated in the manifest and unmanifest, divided and undivided, in the self; stable, most subtle, essence; I worship the destroyer of terrible sins. I have come to Gayā, O god, for the ancestral rite; you be my witness here; I am free from the three debts.'
Let the gods Brahmā, Īśāna, etc., be my witnesses. Having reached Gayā, I have accomplished the redemption of the ancestors.
By reciting the Gayā Māhātmya at the time of śrāddha, one attains the world of Brahman; the śrāddha becomes imperishable for the ancestors and gives the world of Brahman.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Śrāddhakalpaḥ', provides a detailed manual for performing śrāddha rituals, including the selection of Brahmins, the procedure for offerings to ancestors (pitṛs), the use of mantras, and the types of food that grant specific durations of satisfaction. It also covers special śrāddhas like ekoddiṣṭa, sapiṇḍīkaraṇa, and ābhyudayika, along with the benefits of performing śrāddha on specific lunar days.

story.title: Śrāddha Ritual Manual
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; procedural and ritualistic.

teachings.teaching: Śrāddha should be performed at Gayā and during saṅkrānti, especially in the dark fortnight after the fourth lunar day.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of aligning ritual actions with cosmic cycles to harmonize inner energies.

teachings.teaching: Invite Brahmins who are ascetics, householders, sages, or learned in the Vedas, and avoid those with certain defects.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of sattvic association for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The performer should observe celibacy, avoid anger, be truthful, and control speech on the day of śrāddha.
teachings.lens_note: These are foundational yamas and niyamas in Kriya-Yoga, essential for purifying the mind.

teachings.teaching: Offerings to ancestors with specific mantras and procedures grant them satisfaction and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a form of karma yoga where ritual actions, when done with awareness, dissolve karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: Different types of food grant different durations of satisfaction to the ancestors, from one month to twelve years.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of subtle energy (prana) in food affecting consciousness, a key concept in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the story of the seven hunters and ten deer at the beginning of śrāddha makes the ritual complete and grants Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: This narrative serves as a mnemonic for the power of intention; in Kriya-Yoga, the story symbolizes the transformation of lower instincts into higher consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the land of Bharata (India) as a karmabhumi, the land of action, situated north of the ocean and south of the Himalayas, extending nine thousand yojanas. It is the place where beings can attain heaven or liberation through their actions.

story.title: Description of Bharata
story.arc: Agni describes the geographical and spiritual significance of Bharata as the land of action.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; general description.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely descriptive.

teachings.teaching: Bharata is the land of action (karmabhumi) where one can attain heaven or liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this emphasizes that the physical world is the field for spiritual practice (kriya yoga), where disciplined action leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the geography of the seven continents (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, as narrated by Agni. It details Jambūdvīpa, Plakṣadvīpa, Śālmala, Krauñca, Śāka, and Puṣkara, including their dimensions, rulers, mountains, rivers, and inhabitants. The chapter also mentions the Lokāloka mountain and the cosmic egg.

story.title: Description of the Continents and Oceans
story.arc: Cosmological geography
story.timeline_note: Timeless cosmological description
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily descriptive and geographical.

teachings.teaching: The earth consists of seven concentric dvīpas separated by oceans of different liquids, each with its own mountains, rivers, and inhabitants who worship specific deities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this geography symbolizes the layers of consciousness and the progressive refinement of the practitioner's inner landscape, with each dvīpa representing a stage of spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: The oceans are made of milk, curds, ghee, sugarcane juice, and sweet water, and their waters do not increase or decrease.
teachings.lens_note: This constancy of the oceans reflects the unchanging nature of the Self (Ātman) amidst the flux of phenomena, a key insight in Kriya-Yoga meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lokāloka mountain surrounds the world, and beyond it is darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Lokāloka represents the boundary between the manifest and unmanifest, akin to the veil of ignorance that the Kriya-Yoga practitioner transcends through disciplined practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the cosmology of the universe according to the Agni Purana, including the dimensions of the earth, the seven netherworlds (patalas), the planetary system, the sun's chariot, the moon's chariot, and the ultimate reality as Vishnu. It also explains the relationship between Vishnu and the universe, emphasizing that all existence is a manifestation of Vishnu's power.

story.title: Bhuvanakosha: Cosmology of the Universe
story.arc: Exposition of the structure of the cosmos from the netherworlds to the highest heavens, culminating in the identification of all with Vishnu.
story.timeline_note: Cosmic description; no specific narrative time.
story.comic_potential: Visualizing the layered universe and the chariots of the planets could be depicted in a grand cosmic diagram.

teachings.teaching: The universe is a manifestation of Vishnu's power; all existence is rooted in Vishnu.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization that the entire cosmos is a projection of the inner Self (Atman), and through disciplined practice, one can perceive the unity behind diversity.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge of the cosmic order leads to liberation and happiness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that understanding the macrocosm helps in understanding the microcosm of the body; the study of cosmology is a form of yoga that aligns the individual with the universal.

teachings.teaching: Vishnu is the supreme reality, beyond which nothing exists.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of merging individual consciousness with the supreme consciousness, where all dualities dissolve.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 121) is a treatise on Jyotisha (astrology), detailing auspicious and inauspicious timings for various life events such as marriage, upanayana, house construction, agriculture, and more. It includes rules based on nakshatras, tithis, planetary positions, and other astrological factors.

story.title: Jyotisha Shastra
story.arc: Instruction on astrology by Agni
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic text.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical astrological rules.

teachings.teaching: Astrology (Jyotisha) is the essence of the four lakh (400,000) verses and knowing it makes one all-knowing.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that understanding cosmic rhythms (jyotisha) is essential for aligning one's actions with the divine order, leading to spiritual mastery.

teachings.teaching: Marriage should not be performed in the 6th or 8th house from the natal moon, nor in the 2nd or 12th for women, nor in trikona (1st, 5th, 9th) unless there is mutual affection; the 7th house is favorable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as aligning personal relationships with cosmic harmony; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that external unions reflect inner alignment of energies.

teachings.teaching: When Jupiter or Venus are combust (astamita), the person dies or suffers.
teachings.lens_note: Combustion represents the obscuration of wisdom (Jupiter) or love (Venus) by the ego (Sun); Kriya-Yoga teaches that inner clarity is lost when the ego dominates.

teachings.teaching: Do not perform marriage or initiation during Jupiter's retrograde or combustion; wait for three pakshas (fortnights) after combustion and four months after retrograde.
teachings.lens_note: Retrograde and combustion periods are times of introspection; Kriya-Yoga advises avoiding external ceremonies during internal recalibration.

teachings.teaching: Auspicious times for various rites: ear-piercing, tonsure, upanayana, house entry, etc., are given based on nakshatras and weekdays.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as practical guidelines for synchronizing individual karma with universal rhythms, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on timing in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The nine tārās (janma, sampat, vipat, kṣema, pratyari, sādhaka, nidhana, mitra, paramitra) determine the effects of nakshatras on the native.
teachings.lens_note: These tārās represent different karmic influences; in Kriya-Yoga, understanding them helps one navigate life's challenges with awareness.

teachings.teaching: During solar eclipse, all water is as holy as Ganga, all brahmins are like Brahma, and all gold is like Meru.
teachings.lens_note: Eclipses are powerful times for spiritual practice; Sharma's Kriya-Yoga encourages using such moments for deep meditation and charity.

teachings.teaching: The daśās (planetary periods) of Sun, Moon, Mars, and Mercury are given: Sun 6 years, Moon 15, Mars 8, Mercury 17.
teachings.lens_note: These periods are karmic cycles; Kriya-Yoga teaches that awareness of one's daśā helps in focusing spiritual efforts appropriately.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a detailed method for calculating time (kāla gaṇana) based on astronomical cycles, including tithis, nakṣatras, and planetary periods. It provides formulas for determining lunar days, asterisms, and corrections for ṛṇa (deficit) and dhana (excess). The text is highly technical, focusing on computational rules for calendrical astronomy.

story.title: Kāla Gaṇana: The Computation of Time
story.arc: Technical exposition on astronomical calculations for determining tithis, nakṣatras, and planetary periods.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a standalone instructional text without narrative progression.
story.comic_potential: Low; the content is purely mathematical and lacks dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: Time is computed by multiplying the solar year by the number of months starting from Caitra, and then applying various multipliers and divisors to derive tithis and nakṣatras.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this meticulous calculation symbolizes the disciplined measurement of one's spiritual progress through consistent practice (kriya). The precision required mirrors the exactness needed in yogic techniques.

teachings.teaching: The process involves adjusting for deficits (ṛṇa) and surpluses (dhana) in the lunar calendar, with specific rules for different tithis and nakṣatras.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret ṛṇa and dhana as the yogi's need to balance effort and rest, action and surrender. The corrections represent the fine-tuning of one's sadhana to align with natural rhythms.

teachings.teaching: The calculation of nakṣatra involves mixing tithi and asterism, with specific multipliers and divisions to achieve harmony between solar and lunar cycles.
teachings.lens_note: This synthesis of solar and lunar elements reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of balancing the pingala and ida nadis, leading to the awakening of sushumna.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 123) is titled 'Nānāyogāḥ' (Various Yogas) and focuses on astrological and ritual practices for victory in battle. It describes the calculation of time units (nāḍī, pala, liptā), the effects of planetary positions (Śani, Rāhu), the Śanicakra, Kūrmacakra, Rāhucakra, and the classification of muhūrtas (auspicious moments) for various activities. It also provides mantras and recipes for tilaka (forehead mark) and lepa (ointment) to achieve control over others, including gods and humans.

story.title: Nānāyogāḥ (Various Yogas)
story.arc: Instructional arc on astrological and ritual methods for victory
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: The chapter provides methods for calculating auspicious times (muhūrtas) based on planetary positions and tithis, and prescribes specific rituals and mantras for victory in battle.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, the precise calculation of time and alignment with cosmic rhythms is seen as a form of external kriya (action) that harmonizes the practitioner's will with the divine order, facilitating success in worldly endeavors as a reflection of inner spiritual alignment.

teachings.teaching: The chapter describes the use of mantras, tilaka (forehead marks), and lepa (ointments) made from specific herbs and substances to gain control over others, including gods and humans.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, such external applications are considered supportive practices (anga) that purify and energize the body, making it a fit instrument for higher meditation. The use of mantras and herbs is seen as a way to channel prana and align the subtle body with cosmic forces.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yuddhajayārṇavīyajyotiḥśāstrasāra', presents a discourse on the essence of astrology and mantra for victory in battle. It begins with Agni speaking to the sage, then the Devi asks how the gods defeated the demons. Ishvara explains the origin of the fifteen-syllable Shakti mantra, the five primary mantras (Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva, and the Sadyojata group), and the phonemes (a, i, u, e, o) as the root of all. He describes the subtle anatomy of sound, the chakras, and the process of meditation on the white form emitting nectar for immortality and victory. The chapter concludes with a method for applying mantras on the body and the assurance of victory in battle.

story.title: Yuddhajayārṇavīyajyotiḥśāstrasāra
story.arc: Discourse on the essence of astrology and mantra for victory in battle
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a dialogue within the Agni Purana, likely set in a timeless mythological context.
story.comic_potential: The abstract description of mantras and subtle anatomy may be challenging to visualize, but the meditation on the white form emitting nectar and the application of mantras for victory could be depicted symbolically.

teachings.teaching: The fifteen-syllable Shakti mantra is the root of all creation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the activation of the kundalini shakti through the fifteen-syllable mantra, which awakens the subtle energies.

teachings.teaching: The five primary mantras (Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva, and Sadyojata) are the life and death of all mantras.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these mantras correspond to the five pranas and the five elements, and their proper intonation is crucial for pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: The phonemes a, i, u, e, o are the root of all mantras and represent the five kalas.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these vowels are chanted in specific sequences to purify the nadis and balance the chakras.

teachings.teaching: The subtle sound (nada) arises from the heart and the ardhacandra (half-moon) indicates the path to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that meditation on the inner sound (nada) leads to the dissolution of the mind and realization of the Self.

teachings.teaching: Meditate on the white form emitting nectar, contemplating oneself as immortal.
teachings.lens_note: This is a classic Kriya-Yoga visualization: the practitioner visualizes the body as a vessel of amrita (nectar), leading to rejuvenation and victory over death.

teachings.teaching: Apply the Mrityunjaya mantra on the body and worship it before battle to become victorious.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a practical technique for warriors: the mantra is used to protect the body's energy centers, ensuring fearlessness and success.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yuddhajayārṇavīyanānācakrāṇi' (Various Wheels for Victory in War), is a discourse by Lord Śiva on occult and astrological techniques to ensure victory in battle. It includes mantras, deity invocations, cakra (wheel) systems, tithi-yoga combinations, planetary influences, and rituals such as homa (fire offerings) using gruesome materials. The chapter also describes protective herbs and the use of Hanumān's image to break enemy formations.

story.title: Yuddhajayārṇavīyanānācakrāṇi
story.arc: Discourse on occult and astrological methods for victory in war
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual of rituals and astrology.

teachings.teaching: The Karṇamoṭī mantra, when recited with anger, can cause death, downfall, delusion, or expulsion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the power of focused prana and intention (sankalpa) to influence external reality. The mantra is a tool to channel the subtle energy of the practitioner's will.

teachings.teaching: The five 'udayas' (risings) based on svarodaya (sound/breath) are taught; the vital air moves between navel and heart, and can be used to cause expulsion or break enemy's senses in battle.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a description of prana vayu's movement in the subtle body. Controlling the breath (svarodaya) allows one to manipulate the five elements and influence outcomes.

teachings.teaching: By fixing the mind on the subtle essence of smell (gandha) in the nose, one can stupefy (stambhana) enemies without doubt.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on sense withdrawal (pratyahara) and concentration (dharana) on subtle tattvas to gain siddhis.

teachings.teaching: The thirty-two mothers (deities) are stationed in the cakras in groups of eight; they preside over various functions like uccāṭana (expulsion) and stambhana (stupefaction).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these deities as personifications of the energies (shaktis) within the cakras. Invoking them through mantra activates specific psychic centers.

teachings.teaching: The one sun, one moon, and one śakti are differentiated by the diversity of beings, just as water is the same but appears different in various places.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the non-dualistic philosophy of Kriya-Yoga: the underlying unity of consciousness (Brahman) manifests as multiplicity through the prism of the five elements and prana.

teachings.teaching: One should drink the supreme nectar (paramāmṛta) through the brahmāṇḍa kapāla (skull of the cosmic egg), which is covered by the cloth of tattvas.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is a metaphor for piercing the brahmarandhra (crown chakra) to access the immortal bliss of the Self. The 'cloth of tattvas' refers to the veils of the five elements.

teachings.teaching: Victory in war depends on the strength of the five groups (pañcavarga) of letters; the five vargas (ka, ca, ṭa, ta, pa) are described.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would relate this to the bija mantras of the five elements. Proper intonation and mental focus on these syllables can harmonize the elements for success.

teachings.teaching: The tithi-yoga (lunar day-planet combinations) determine outcomes: e.g., Kṛttikā on pratipad (first day) with Mars brings gain; Ṣaṣṭhī with Mars and Maghā brings pain.
teachings.lens_note: This is a practical application of astrological timing. In Kriya-Yoga, aligning actions with cosmic rhythms (ṛta) enhances the efficacy of spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The eight directions (dṛṣṭi) starting from Brahmā are associated with tithis; when combined with zodiac signs, they lead to success.
teachings.lens_note: These dṛṣṭis are 'gazes' or aspects. In Kriya-Yoga, directional orientation during meditation can influence the flow of prana.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 126) provides a detailed astrological guide for determining auspicious and inauspicious times based on nakshatras (lunar mansions), planetary positions, and other celestial factors. It covers topics such as the effects of the Sun and Rahu in various nakshatras, classification of nakshatras into fixed, movable, mild, and fierce types, and rules for selecting favorable times for activities like travel, construction, and warfare. The chapter also discusses 'dagdha' (burnt) tithis and 'tripushkara' yogas, which are considered inauspicious.

story.title: Nakshatra Nirnaya: Determination of Auspicious Times
story.arc: Instructional discourse by Ishvara on astrological principles.
story.timeline_note: Timeless teaching; no narrative sequence.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical exposition.

teachings.teaching: The classification of nakshatras into fixed (sthira), movable (kshipra), mild (mridu), and fierce (dāruṇa) types determines their suitability for various activities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this classification mirrors the inner states of consciousness: fixed for stable meditation, movable for dynamic practices, mild for compassionate actions, fierce for overcoming obstacles.

teachings.teaching: The positions of Sun and Rahu in nakshatras influence victory and defeat in battle, gambling, and travel.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the interplay of inner light (Sun) and shadow (Rahu) in the practitioner's psyche; aligning actions with favorable inner states leads to success.

teachings.teaching: Certain tithis (lunar days) are 'dagdha' (burnt) by specific planets, making them inauspicious for starting new ventures.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these correspond to times when the mind is clouded by planetary influences; one should refrain from initiating important practices during such periods.

teachings.teaching: The 'tripushkara' yoga, involving specific nakshatras and tithis, multiplies the effects of actions, both good and bad.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that during such yogas, the karmic results are amplified; thus, one should engage only in virtuous actions to maximize spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The 'gaṇḍānta' periods at the junctions of nakshatras are dangerous and should be avoided for auspicious activities.
teachings.lens_note: These transitional times represent instability in the subtle energy currents; Kriya-Yoga advises meditation and inner focus rather than external actions.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses astrological considerations for determining auspicious and inauspicious times, particularly for activities like travel, war, and marriage. It covers the effects of planetary positions, lunar mansions (nakshatras), and the characteristics of zodiac signs (fixed, movable, dual). The chapter also includes rules for avoiding certain muhurtas (auspicious moments) based on the yoga (combination) and planetary placements.

story.title: Nānābalāni: Various Strengths and Astrological Considerations
story.arc: Instructional discourse on astrology for determining auspicious timings
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Agni Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily technical astrological rules.

teachings.teaching: Avoid certain muhurtas like Vishkumbha, Shula, Ganda, etc., for travel and war.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of aligning actions with cosmic rhythms to minimize obstacles and maximize spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Planetary positions in different houses (from the birth sign) determine auspiciousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a reminder that external circumstances reflect inner states; favorable planetary alignments support inner discipline and yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Zodiac signs have fixed, movable, or dual natures, affecting the outcome of actions.
teachings.lens_note: This classification mirrors the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and guides the practitioner to choose activities that harmonize with their inherent nature.

teachings.teaching: Rain in certain nakshatras indicates prolonged or brief rainfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as a metaphor for the duration of spiritual practices: consistent effort (like rain in Yamabha) yields lasting results.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the construction and interpretation of the Koṭacakra, a diagram used for determining auspiciousness in warfare and other endeavors. It details the placement of nakshatras (lunar mansions) in concentric rings and the central area, and explains how the positions of planets and nakshatras influence victory or defeat.

story.title: The Koṭacakra: A Diagram for Victory
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of the Agni Purana's teachings on military astrology.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical and diagrammatic.

teachings.teaching: The Koṭacakra diagram, with its concentric squares and placement of nakshatras, determines the outcome of battles and other ventures based on planetary and stellar positions.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this external diagram symbolizes the internal alignment of energies (prana) within the practitioner. The 'fort' (durgasya) represents the body, and the nakshatras correspond to energy centers (chakras). Victory in battle mirrors mastery over inner impulses.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the arghakāṇḍa, a ritual offering based on lunar months and omens such as meteors, earthquakes, eclipses, and directional fires. It specifies the types of offerings (grains, ghee, cloth, etc.) to be made in each month to achieve prosperity.

story.title: Arghakāṇḍa: The Ritual Offering
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: When omens like meteors, earthquakes, eclipses, or directional fires occur, one should perform the arghakāṇḍa offering with specific items according to the month to attain prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes aligning outer rituals with inner discipline. The prescribed offerings symbolize the purification of the senses and mind, turning external events into opportunities for spiritual growth.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Ghātacakra, a system of astrological circles (maṇḍalas) used for predicting victory or defeat based on planetary and celestial omens. It details the Āgneya maṇḍala (fire circle) and its associated portents, such as earthquakes, eclipses, comets, and unusual weather, which indicate destruction of specific regions and suffering for the populace. The chapter also mentions the Vāyavya maṇḍala (wind circle) and provides rules for interpreting lunar positions in relation to asterisms.

story.title: Ghātacakra: The Circle of Destruction
story.arc: Instruction on astrological omens for victory
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical astrological content.

teachings.teaching: Observing celestial and terrestrial omens (earthquakes, eclipses, comets, etc.) allows prediction of regional destruction and societal suffering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the interconnectedness of cosmic and human realms, where external signs reflect internal spiritual states. The practitioner is urged to cultivate inner stability to withstand outer disturbances.

teachings.teaching: The Āgneya maṇḍala (fire circle) consists of specific asterisms and portents indicating destruction of certain regions and classes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against the fiery passions (krodha, lobha) that lead to collective downfall. The maṇḍala serves as a map of karmic consequences.

teachings.teaching: When the moon transits the fifteenth asterism (Bhānoḥ pañcadaśe hy ṛkṣe), it indicates the destruction of Somagrāma.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of timing in Kriya-Yoga: specific planetary alignments correspond to energetic shifts that can be harnessed for spiritual progress or avoided for protection.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 131) describes various astrological and numerological methods for predicting victory or defeat in battle, including the construction and interpretation of cakras (circles) based on syllables, tithis, and nakshatras. It details the ghātacakra (killing circle), naracakra (human circle), and jayacakra (victory circle), providing rules for determining auspicious and inauspicious outcomes based on the positions of planets and the length of names.

story.title: Ghātacakrādi: On Circles of Killing and Victory
story.arc: Instructional discourse on battle omens
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; purely prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical and astrological.

teachings.teaching: The cakra (circle) is drawn by writing vowels from a to au in clockwise order starting from the east, and then the months from Caitra onward are placed by rotation. The tithis from pratipad to pūrṇimā are assigned.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this external cakra mirrors the internal cycle of prāṇa and consciousness; aligning actions with cosmic rhythms leads to victory.

teachings.teaching: In the Caitra cakra, contact (saṃsparśa) determines victory or gain; odd numbers are auspicious, even numbers inauspicious.
teachings.lens_note: Odd numbers represent the active, dynamic principle (rajas) leading to success; even numbers represent inertia (tamas) leading to failure.

teachings.teaching: At the time of battle, if the name of a warrior is uttered and that name is mounted on a vowel (svarārūḍha), and if the Sun or Guru is in that vowel, then that warrior always wins.
teachings.lens_note: The name is a vibration; when aligned with the solar or guru principle, it becomes invincible. In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the power of mantra and divine will.

teachings.teaching: If a warrior has a short name (hrasva), he dies inevitably; if the first syllable is long (dīrgha) and placed at the beginning, the second in the middle, and the third at the end, then two (victories) occur with the middle and first-last.
teachings.lens_note: Length of syllables corresponds to prāṇic duration; short names indicate weak prāṇa, long names indicate strong prāṇa leading to victory.

teachings.teaching: The naracakra (human circle) is described: draw an image, place nakshatras on its parts (head, face, eyes, hands, ears, heart, feet). Place the name's nakshatra in the center of the circle.
teachings.lens_note: The human body is a microcosm; the cakra maps cosmic energies onto the body. In Kriya-Yoga, this is the subtle body's chakra system.

teachings.teaching: If the Sun, Saturn, Mars, or Rāhu are stationed in a particular body part (eye, head, right ear, right hand, foot, etc.), then destruction (ghāta) occurs in that place.
teachings.lens_note: Malefic planets in certain body parts indicate blockages in the corresponding prāṇic centers, leading to defeat or harm.

teachings.teaching: The jayacakra (victory circle) is drawn with thirteen vertical lines and six horizontal lines, then filled with syllables from a to ha, and with directions, planets, sages, suns, priests, rudras, and tithis in order.
teachings.lens_note: This cakra integrates all cosmic forces; meditating on it aligns the practitioner with the universal order, ensuring victory.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Sevācakra (Service Wheel), a divinatory system for assessing gain and loss in relationships. It includes instructions for constructing a grid of 35 cells, assigning letters and categories (deities, demons, etc.), and interpreting results based on planetary positions, star constellations (Tārācakra), and zodiac signs. The chapter also discusses friendship and enmity between signs.

story.title: Sevācakra: The Wheel of Service
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: The Sevācakra is a tool to predict gain or loss in relationships, based on a grid of 35 cells and the placement of letters representing different classes of beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of svādhyāya (self-study) and the use of external symbols to understand internal karmic patterns. The grid serves as a map of one's mental and emotional landscape, where each cell corresponds to a vṛtti (mental modification).

teachings.teaching: The classification of beings into deities, demons, serpents, gandharvas, sages, rākṣasas, piśācas, and humans, with a hierarchy of strength.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a representation of the guṇas (qualities) and the levels of consciousness. The stronger beings (deities) correspond to sattva, while weaker (humans) to tamas. The practitioner should align with sattvic forces.

teachings.teaching: The Tārācakra (star wheel) determines auspiciousness based on the birth star and the nine tārās (stars): janma, sampat, vipat, kṣema, pratyara, dhanadā, naidhana, maitra, paramaitra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a method to discern the timing of actions in harmony with cosmic rhythms, akin to the practice of kriyā-yoga where one aligns with the natural flow of prāṇa.

teachings.teaching: Friendship and enmity between zodiac signs are described, advising against serving enemies.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches discernment (viveka) in relationships, a key aspect of kriyā-yoga where one avoids associations that disturb mental equilibrium.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Nānābalāni' (Various Armies), provides astrological and ritual instructions for achieving victory in battle. It describes the characteristics of individuals born under different planetary influences, the effects of planetary periods (daśā), and various mantras, yantras, and practices such as the Tārkṣya (Garuda) cakra, Picchikā, and Mṛtyuñjaya for overcoming enemies and ensuring success in warfare.

story.title: Nānābalāni (Various Armies)
story.arc: Instructional discourse on astrological and ritual means to victory in battle
story.timeline_note: No specific narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical manual of astrology and ritual.

teachings.teaching: The characteristics of individuals born under different planetary houses determine their nature and fortune.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that one's inherent tendencies (vāsanās) are shaped by past actions and can be transformed through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The effects of planetary periods (daśā) bring various results such as wealth, land, or disease.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the karmic fruition of actions; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes conscious action to mitigate negative daśā.

teachings.teaching: Chanting mantras and performing rituals with proper visualization can cause enemy armies to flee.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the power of sound and intention in Kriya-Yoga; mantra repetition (japa) purifies the mind and aligns one with cosmic forces.

teachings.teaching: The Tārkṣya (Garuda) cakra and related practices grant invincibility and victory.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the ascent of consciousness; Garuda represents the divine will that overcomes obstacles.

teachings.teaching: The Mṛtyuñjaya yantra and mantra overcome death and enemies.
teachings.lens_note: Mṛtyuñjaya (conqueror of death) in Kriya-Yoga signifies mastery over the cycle of birth and death through self-realization.

chapter_summary: Lord Shiva describes the Trailokyavijaya Vidya, a powerful mantra and ritual for overcoming obstacles, subduing enemies, and gaining control over the three worlds. The chapter includes the mantra, visualization of the goddess, and instructions for nyasa and homa.

story.title: Trailokyavijaya Vidya
story.arc: Instruction on a powerful mantra and ritual for victory and control.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of Agni Purana teachings.
story.comic_potential: Visualizing the fierce goddess with twenty arms and performing rituals could be depicted dramatically.

teachings.teaching: The Trailokyavijaya mantra and ritual can subdue all enemies and grant control over the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the power of focused intention and divine invocation to overcome internal and external obstacles, aligning with the practice of Kriya to master the mind and environment.

teachings.teaching: Nyasa and homa with red flowers are prescribed for success in battle.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the purification and energizing of the subtle body through ritual actions, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents the Saṅgrāmavijayavidyā, a mantra and ritual practice for victory in battle. It includes a long invocation to the goddess Cāmuṇḍā, describing her fearsome form and powers, and prescribes the visualization of a 28-armed deity. The practice involves recitation, homa (fire sacrifice), and other rites to ensure success in war.

story.title: Saṅgrāmavijayavidyā
story.arc: Instruction on a mantra and ritual for victory in battle, given by Īśvara (Śiva).
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of the goddess's terrifying form and the series of commands could be depicted as a powerful ritual scene.

teachings.teaching: Recitation and homa of this mantra, along with visualization of the 28-armed deity, grants victory in battle.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching emphasizes the power of mantra and visualization to align inner energies with divine forces, achieving success through focused intention and ritual action.
