chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Prastāranirūpaṇa' (Exposition of Prosody), provides a technical exposition on metrics (chandas) and the method of prastāra (arrangement of syllables). It describes rules for determining the structure of verses, including the use of guru (heavy) and laghu (light) syllables, and the meruprastāra method for calculating combinations.

story.title: Prastāranirūpaṇa
story.arc: Technical exposition on prosody
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic section.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical content.

teachings.teaching: The method of prastāra: starting with all guru syllables, then progressively replacing with laghu, and using meruprastāra to compute combinations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this systematic arrangement of syllables mirrors the disciplined structuring of breath and mantra in yoga practice, where each syllable corresponds to a specific energy pattern.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 335) is titled 'Śikṣānirūpaṇam' (Exposition of Phonetics). It describes the science of pronunciation and phonetics, including the classification of vowels, consonants, their places of articulation, and the proper method of utterance for achieving spiritual merit.

story.title: Śikṣānirūpaṇam
story.arc: The chapter is a technical exposition on phonetics, not a narrative. It is part of the Agni Purana's teachings on ritual and language.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic section.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual on pronunciation.

teachings.teaching: Proper pronunciation of sounds (varṇas) leads to attainment of the Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this emphasizes the importance of precise articulation in mantra practice, aligning breath and sound to activate subtle energies.

teachings.teaching: The process of sound production involves mind, body, and breath: 'manaḥ kāyāgnimāhanti sa prerayati mārutam'.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of using breath (prāṇa) to control the mind and body, linking internal fire (kāyāgni) with vocalization.

teachings.teaching: Classification of sounds by place of articulation (sthāna) and effort (prayatna) is essential for correct chanting.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would stress that precise articulation purifies the nadis and balances the five elements within the body.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the characteristics and classification of poetry (kāvya) and drama (nāṭaka). It covers definitions of dhvani, varṇa, pada, vākya, and the three types of kāvya: prose (gadya), verse (padya), and mixed (miśra). It elaborates on sub-types of prose, the structure of mahākāvya, and various poetic forms like kalāpa, kulaka, muktaka, and koṣa. The chapter emphasizes the importance of rasa (aesthetic emotion) as the life of poetry.

story.title: Characteristics of Poetry and Drama
story.arc: Exposition of literary theory
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Poetry is defined as words and meanings adorned with figures of speech, possessing qualities and devoid of defects.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need for disciplined expression (vāk) as a means to refine consciousness; the 'adornment' corresponds to the purification of thought and speech.

teachings.teaching: Rasa (aesthetic emotion) is the life of poetry, even though verbal skill is primary.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret rasa as the experiential essence (ananda) that arises from disciplined practice; the poet's craft is a form of yoga that channels universal emotions.

teachings.teaching: The three types of poetry: prose, verse, and mixed, correspond to different modes of expression.
teachings.lens_note: This classification mirrors the threefold path of Kriya-Yoga: physical (kriya), verbal (japa), and mental (dhyāna); each has its place in spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: A mahākāvya should be based on itihāsa or other noble themes, with descriptions of cities, oceans, seasons, etc., and should be rich in all rasas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as an allegory for the complete spiritual journey: the city as the body, the ocean as the unconscious, seasons as cycles of practice, and rasas as the flavors of divine experience.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana enumerates and defines the 27 types of dramatic performance (rūpaka), including nāṭaka, prakaraṇa, ḍima, etc. It discusses the general and specific characteristics of drama, the importance of rasa, the structure of the pūrvaraṅga (preliminaries), and the components such as nāndī, sūtradhāra, and the invocation of deities and gurus.

story.title: Enumeration of Dramatic Forms
story.arc: Instruction on the 27 types of drama and their characteristics.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical treatise.

teachings.teaching: Drama is a means to achieve the three aims of life (trivarga: dharma, artha, kāma).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this aligns with the integration of worldly and spiritual goals through disciplined action (kriya).

teachings.teaching: The general characteristics (sāmānya) apply to all dramas, while specific characteristics (viśeṣa) apply to particular types.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga principle of universal practices (sāmānya) and individualized techniques (viśeṣa) for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The pūrvaraṅga includes invocations to deities, gurus, cows, brahmins, and kings, and the sūtradhāra introduces the play.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the importance of reverence and proper preparation, akin to the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on purification and invocation before practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the theory of rasa (aesthetic emotion) in drama and poetry. It begins by describing the ultimate reality as the supreme Brahman, from which arises the sense of ego (ahaṅkāra), then abhimāna (self-importance), and then rati (love), which when fully developed becomes śṛṅgāra (erotic rasa). It enumerates the eight primary rasas: śṛṅgāra, hāsya, karuṇa, raudra, vīra, bhayānaka, vībhatsa, adbhuta, and śānta. It explains how rasas arise from the interplay of sattva, rajas, and tamas, and from specific emotions (sthāyibhāvas) like rati, hāsa, śoka, etc. It also describes the thirty-three vyabhicāribhāvas (transitory emotions), the four types of nāyakas (heroes), and the roles of pīṭhamarda, viṭa, and vidūṣaka as companions. The chapter emphasizes that a poet creates a world of rasa, and without rasa, poetry is lifeless.

story.title: Śṛṅgārādirasanirūpaṇam
story.arc: Exposition of the theory of rasa in drama and poetry
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical treatise on aesthetics.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Brahman is the ultimate reality, from which all rasas arise through a process of evolution involving ahaṅkāra and abhimāna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this aligns with the idea that the ultimate consciousness (Brahman) manifests as the ego (ahaṅkāra) and then as the sense of self-importance (abhimāna), which gives rise to the aesthetic emotion (rasa) as a reflection of divine bliss.

teachings.teaching: The eight primary rasas (śṛṅgāra, hāsya, karuṇa, raudra, vīra, bhayānaka, vībhatsa, adbhuta) and śānta arise from the interplay of the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and specific emotions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a map of the inner landscape: the guṇas condition the mind, and the rasas are the experiential flavors that arise from their interaction. Kriya-Yoga aims to transcend these by realizing the pure consciousness beyond.

teachings.teaching: A poet creates a world of rasa; if the poet is passionate (śṛṅgārī), the world becomes rasa-filled; if dispassionate (vītarāga), it becomes devoid of rasa.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes Sharma's emphasis on the inner state of the practitioner: the quality of one's consciousness determines the experience. In Kriya-Yoga, cultivating inner purity and devotion (bhakti) makes one's perception of the world infused with divine rasa.

teachings.teaching: The thirty-three vyabhicāribhāvas (transitory emotions) are enumerated, including stambha, sveda, romāñca, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These correspond to subtle bodily and mental changes that accompany deep emotional states. In Kriya-Yoga, such phenomena are observed as signs of prāṇic and spiritual awakening during meditation.

teachings.teaching: The nāyaka (hero) is of four types: dhīrodātta, dhīroddhata, dhīralalita, and dhīrapraśānta.
teachings.lens_note: These archetypes represent different modes of spiritual temperament. In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner may embody these qualities at different stages: the steady (dhīra) and the passionate (udātta) etc.

teachings.teaching: The companions of the hero in śṛṅgāra are pīṭhamarda, viṭa, and vidūṣaka.
teachings.lens_note: These characters facilitate the unfolding of rasa. In the inner drama of yoga, the mind's faculties (buddhi, manas, ahaṅkāra) play similar roles in the experience of divine love.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the concept of 'rīti' (style) in literary composition, describing four types: Pāñcālī, Gauḍī, Vaidarbhī, and Lāṭajā. It also elaborates on the four 'vṛttis' (dramatic styles): Bhāratī, Ārabhaṭī, Kauśikī, and Sātvatī, with details on their characteristics and components.

story.title: Rīti-nirūpaṇa (Description of Styles)
story.arc: Exposition of literary and dramatic theory
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical treatise.

teachings.teaching: Rīti (style) is fourfold: Pāñcālī, Gauḍī, Vaidarbhī, and Lāṭajā, each with distinct characteristics of upacāra (ornamentation), sandarbha (composition), and vigraha (structure).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this classification mirrors the four stages of yogic practice: Pāñcālī corresponds to initial effort (mṛdvī), Gauḍī to unstable energy (anavasthita), Vaidarbhī to balanced refinement (nātikomalā), and Lāṭajā to clear but not overly sharp awareness (sphuṭa).

teachings.teaching: Vṛtti (dramatic style) is fourfold: Bhāratī (speech-dominated), Ārabhaṭī (magic and battle), Kauśikī (graceful), and Sātvatī (heroic).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as modes of inner action: Bhāratī as mantra repetition, Ārabhaṭī as forceful prāṇāyāma, Kauśikī as subtle meditation, and Sātvatī as steady devotion.

teachings.teaching: Bhāratī has four aṅgas (limbs): vīthī, prahasana, prastāvanā, and nāṭaka; vīthī has thirteen sub-types including udghātaka, lapita, asatpralāpa, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These dramatic elements correspond to stages of inner dialogue in yoga: udghātaka (sudden insight), lapita (whispered intention), asatpralāpa (discarding false thoughts), etc.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 340) describes the various movements and gestures (angakarma) used in dance and drama, categorizing them by body parts such as head, eyebrows, eyes, nose, lips, chin, neck, hands, chest, abdomen, sides, shanks, and feet. It enumerates specific types of movements and their classifications, providing a technical manual for performance arts.

story.title: Description of Body Movements in Dance and Drama
story.arc: Technical exposition on angakarma (body movements) for performance arts.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: The chapter categorizes body movements (angakarma) into specific types for each body part, essential for proper expression in dance and drama.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this detailed classification of physical movements can be seen as a metaphor for the precise control of prana and subtle energies in yogic practice, where each gesture (mudra) and movement corresponds to specific energetic effects.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the theory of abhinaya (dramatic representation) and the classification of rasas (aesthetic sentiments). It describes the four types of abhinaya: sattvika (mental), vacika (verbal), angika (physical), and aharya (costume). It then elaborates on the srngara (erotic) rasa, its two divisions (sambhoga and vipralambha), and further subdivisions. The chapter also covers hasya (comic), karuna (pathetic), raudra (furious), and vira (heroic) rasas, detailing their causes and manifestations.

story.title: Abhinaya and Rasa Theory
story.arc: Exposition of dramatic principles
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical treatise.

teachings.teaching: Abhinaya is the representation of meaning by facing the audience; it is fourfold: sattvika, vacika, angika, aharya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, abhinaya corresponds to the external expression of inner spiritual states; the four types mirror the yogic disciplines of mind, speech, body, and environment.

teachings.teaching: Srngara (erotic) rasa is twofold: sambhoga (union) and vipralambha (separation); each is further divided into concealed and manifest.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, sambhoga represents the union of individual consciousness with the divine, while vipralambha signifies the longing and discipline of separation leading to that union.

teachings.teaching: Hasya (comic) rasa has four types: smita (gentle smile), hasita (laugh), vihasita (loud laugh), and atihasita (excessive laugh).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret these as stages of spiritual joy: from subtle inner bliss to ecstatic expression.

teachings.teaching: Karuna (pathetic) rasa arises from dharma-upaghata (injury to righteousness) and citta-vilasa (mental agitation); its sthayi (stable emotion) is soka (grief).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, karuna reflects the pain of separation from the divine, which purifies the heart and leads to devotion.

teachings.teaching: Raudra (furious) rasa is threefold: arising from anger expressed through body, costume, and speech.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see raudra as the fierce determination in spiritual practice to overcome obstacles.

teachings.teaching: Vira (heroic) rasa is threefold: dana-vira (charity), dharma-vira (righteousness), and yuddha-vira (war).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, vira represents the courage to pursue spiritual disciplines, give up attachments, and fight inner demons.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses śabdālaṅkāras (verbal figures of speech), including anuprāsa (alliteration), yamaka (paronomasia), citra (pictorial poetry), and various types of bandha (patterns). It provides definitions, classifications, and examples of these poetic devices.

story.title: Śabdālaṅkāras: Verbal Figures of Speech
story.arc: Exposition of poetic devices
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical treatise on poetics.

teachings.teaching: Anuprāsa is the repetition of letters in words or sentences, classified by single or multiple letter repetition, and further into five vṛttis: madhurā, lalitā, prauṭā, bhadrā, and paruṣā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the rhythmic repetition of sounds can be seen as a form of mantra-like focus, aiding in concentration and purification of speech.

teachings.teaching: Yamaka is the repetition of a sequence of letters with different meanings, classified as avyapeta (contiguous) and vyapeta (separated), with further subdivisions based on position in the verse.
teachings.lens_note: The play of multiple meanings through sound repetition mirrors the yogic principle of unity in diversity, where the same sound can lead to different realizations.

teachings.teaching: Citra (pictorial poetry) includes praśna (riddle), prahelikā (enigma), gupta (concealed), cyuta (omitted), datta (added), ubhaya (both), and samasyā (composite verse).
teachings.lens_note: These complex forms challenge the mind to see beyond surface meaning, akin to the yogic practice of discerning the subtle from the gross.

teachings.teaching: Bandha (pattern) includes gomūtrikā (cow urine pattern), sarvatobhadra (all-round auspicious), ambuja (lotus), cakra (wheel), and others, formed by arranging syllables in geometric shapes.
teachings.lens_note: The visual arrangement of syllables into sacred shapes (like lotus or wheel) can be seen as a form of yantra, aiding meditation and symbolizing cosmic order.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana discusses arthālaṅkāras (figures of speech based on meaning). It begins by defining arthālaṅkāra as the ornamentation of meaning, without which even beautiful words are not charming. It then enumerates eight primary types: svarūpa (natural description), sādṛśya (similarity), utprekṣā (poetic fancy), atiśaya (hyperbole), vibhāvanā (causal impossibility), virodha (contradiction), hetu (cause), and sama (equality). Under sādṛśya, it lists four subtypes: upamā (simile), rūpaka (metaphor), mahokti (exaggeration), and arthāntaranyāsa (generalization). The chapter then elaborates on upamā (simile), describing its various forms based on the presence or absence of compounds, the expression of common quality, and the relationship between upamāna and upameya. It details eighteen types of upamā, including dharmavastūpame, parasparopamā, viparītopamā, niyamopamā, aniyamopamā, samuccayopamā, vyatirekopamā, bahūpamā, and mālopamā.

story.title: Arthālaṅkāra Nirūpaṇa
story.arc: Exposition of figures of speech based on meaning
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical discussion of poetics.

teachings.teaching: Arthālaṅkāra is the ornamentation of meaning; without it, even beautiful words are not charming.
Sarasvati without arthālaṅkāra is like a widow.
The eight primary arthālaṅkāras are: svarūpa, sādṛśya, utprekṣā, atiśaya, vibhāvanā, virodha, hetu, and sama.
Svarūpa is the natural description of things, divided into nija (inherent) and āgantuka (accidental).
Sādṛśya (similarity) has four subtypes: upamā, rūpaka, mahokti, and arthāntaranyāsa.
Upamā (simile) is defined as the expression of similarity between upamāna and upameya, even when they share a common quality.
Upamā is twofold: with compound (sasamāsa) and without compound (asamāsa), and further subdivided into eighteen types based on the expression of the common quality and the relationship between terms.
Types of upamā include: dharmavastūpame, parasparopamā, viparītopamā, niyamopamā, aniyamopamā, samuccayopamā, vyatirekopamā, bahūpamā, and mālopamā.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the alaṅkāras (ornaments) of poetry, focusing on śabdālaṅkāra (sound figures) and arthālaṅkāra (sense figures). It defines six aspects of poetic expression: praśasti (praise), kānti (beauty), aucitya (propriety), saṃkṣepa (brevity), yāvadarthatā (adequacy), and abhivyakti (manifestation). It further elaborates on types of śruti (hearing) and the distinction between mukhya (primary) and upacārika (secondary) meanings.

story.title: Alaṅkāraśāstra in the Agni Purāṇa
story.arc: Exposition of poetic ornaments by Agni
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical treatise.

teachings.teaching: Poetic ornaments (alaṅkāras) enhance both sound and sense equally, like a necklace adorns both the breast and neck of a woman.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the harmony of inner and outer practice—the ornamentation of speech mirrors the refinement of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Six aspects of poetic expression: praśasti (praise), kānti (beauty), aucitya (propriety), saṃkṣepa (brevity), yāvadarthatā (adequacy), and abhivyakti (manifestation).
teachings.lens_note: These correspond to the six limbs of yoga (ṣaḍaṅga) in Kriya-Yoga, where each aspect refines the practitioner's expression of truth.

teachings.teaching: Praise (praśasti) is of two types: premokti (affectionate speech) and stuti (eulogy).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, affectionate speech arises from bhakti (devotion), while eulogy is a form of self-surrender (prapatti).

teachings.teaching: Beauty (kānti) is the harmony of all pleasing elements, where the style (rīti) matches the subject, and the sentiment (rasa) aligns with the meter (vṛtti).
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the yogic principle of sāmya (equipoise) where all aspects of practice are in balance.

teachings.teaching: Brevity (saṃkṣepa) is the condensation of much meaning into few words.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this parallels the efficiency of mantra repetition (japa) where a single syllable contains profound spiritual power.

teachings.teaching: Adequacy (yāvadarthatā) is the absence of excess or deficiency in words and meaning.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the yogic concept of madhyamā (the middle path) avoiding extremes in practice.

teachings.teaching: Manifestation (abhivyakti) has two types: śruti (hearing) and ākṣepa (suggestion). Śruti is further divided into naimittikī (occasional) and pāribhāṣikī (technical).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, śruti corresponds to śravaṇa (hearing scriptures), while ākṣepa is akin to intuitive insight (pratibhā).

teachings.teaching: Words have primary (mukhya) and secondary (upacārika) meanings. Secondary meaning arises from a relation (nimitta) and is of two types: lākṣaṇikī (indicative) and gauṇī (qualitative).
teachings.lens_note: This parallels the yogic understanding of direct experience (mukhya) versus symbolic interpretation (upacārika) in meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the qualities (guṇas) of poetry, distinguishing between general and specific qualities, and elaborates on the seven qualities of sound (śabda-guṇas): śleṣa (compactness), lālitya (grace), gāmbhīrya (depth), saukumārya (delicacy), udāratā (nobility), ojas (vigor), and prasāda (clarity). It also touches on the relationship between qualities and figures of speech, and the importance of context in determining whether a feature is a quality or a defect.

story.title: Discussion on the Qualities of Poetry
story.arc: Exposition of literary theory
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger discourse on alaṅkāra (poetics) in the Agni Purana.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Even a decorated poem without qualities (guṇas) does not please; like ornaments on a woman's body that become a burden.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the principle that external practices without inner transformation are futile. The 'qualities' correspond to inner virtues cultivated through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: Qualities like śleṣa (compactness) and others are sevenfold for sound; they are distinct from defects.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a metaphor for the seven stages of yogic purification, where each quality refines the practitioner's expression and perception.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification of poetic faults (doṣas) in kāvya. It enumerates seven types of faults related to the speaker (vaktṛ), the word (vācaka), and the meaning (vācya), and further subdivides them into categories such as asādhutva, aprayuktatva, chāndasatva, avispaṣṭatva, kaṣṭatva, asāmayikatva, and grāmyatva. It also covers common faults like kriyābhraṃśa, visandhi, punarukta, and vyastasambandhatā, and discusses exceptions where certain faults are acceptable in difficult compositions.

story.title: Kāvyadoṣaviveka (Analysis of Poetic Faults)
story.arc: Exposition of literary theory
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: None

teachings.teaching: A fault (doṣa) is that which causes distress to the learned audience. It is sevenfold, arising from the speaker, the word, or the meaning, singly or in combination.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that any action (kriya) that disturbs the inner peace or clarity of the practitioner is a fault. The sevenfold classification mirrors the seven levels of consciousness or vrittis that need to be purified.

teachings.teaching: The speaker (vaktṛ) is the poet, of four types: doubtful, disrespectful, ignorant, and knowledgeable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as stages of the practitioner's mind: doubt (sandihāna) corresponds to uncertainty in practice, disrespect (avinīta) to lack of devotion, ignorance (ajña) to lack of understanding, and knowledge (jñātā) to realized insight.

teachings.teaching: Word faults include asādhutva (incorrectness according to grammar) and aprayuktatva (non-usage by the learned).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, incorrect usage of words reflects imprecise mental formations; the practitioner must align speech with truth (satya) and proper understanding.

teachings.teaching: Meaning faults include gūḍhārthatā (obscure meaning), viparyastārthatā (contrary meaning), saṃśayitārthatā (doubtful meaning), and avispaṣṭārthatā (indistinct meaning).
teachings.lens_note: These correspond to obstacles in comprehending the true nature of reality: obscurity (avidya), perversion (viparyaya), doubt (samshaya), and vagueness (asamprajnata). Kriya-Yoga aims to clear these through direct experience.

teachings.teaching: Kaṣṭatva (difficulty in pronunciation) and asāmayikatva (impropriety of time/context) are faults.
teachings.lens_note: In practice, difficulty in articulation reflects inner resistance; proper timing (kala) is crucial in yogic disciplines for effective practice.

teachings.teaching: Grāmyatva (vulgarity) arises from using vulgar words, from recollection of vulgarity, or from similarity to a vulgar word.
teachings.lens_note: Vulgarity in speech indicates impure mental impressions (vasanas). Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind through mantra and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Common faults (sādhāraṇa doṣa) include kriyābhraṃśa (loss of verb), visandhi (improper sandhi), punarukta (repetition), and vyastasambandhatā (disordered connection).
teachings.lens_note: These reflect structural imbalances in action (kriya), union (sandhi), redundancy, and order—all relevant to the systematic practice of Kriya-Yoga where each element must be correctly placed.

teachings.teaching: Certain faults are excused in difficult compositions (duṣkara) or when they do not cause distress to the learned.
teachings.lens_note: In advanced practice, apparent irregularities may be allowed if they serve a higher purpose, akin to the yogi's transcendence of conventional rules.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 347) is titled 'Ekākṣarābhidhāna' (The One-Syllable Lexicon). It provides a detailed esoteric mapping of Sanskrit phonemes (from 'a' to 'kṣa') to deities, concepts, and cosmic principles. It then prescribes mantras and rituals for worshiping various forms of Durgā, Gaṇeśa, and other deities, emphasizing the power of single-syllable mantras for liberation and worldly benefits.

story.title: Ekākṣarābhidhāna: The One-Syllable Lexicon
story.arc: Ritual and Mantra Section
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual of phoneme-deity correspondences and ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: Each Sanskrit phoneme (from 'a' to 'kṣa') corresponds to a deity or cosmic principle, forming a sacred alphabet that can be used in mantras for spiritual and material benefits.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of mantra yoga where sound vibrations are used to awaken inner energies and connect with specific deities. The phoneme-deity mapping serves as a tool for meditation and concentration.

teachings.teaching: The one-syllable mantra (ekākṣara mantra) is a powerful tool for bestowing both enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the efficiency of concise mantras for rapid spiritual progress. The ekākṣara mantra condenses the essence of a deity into a single syllable, making it a potent focus for meditation.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Durgā and her nine forms (Nava Durgā) through specific mantras and rituals leads to protection and fulfillment of desires.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of invoking divine feminine energy (Śakti) for purification and empowerment. The nine forms represent stages of spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: Gaṇapati worship with specific mantras and offerings (e.g., tilahoma) grants success and removes obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, Gaṇapati is the remover of obstacles on the spiritual path. The prescribed rituals help align the practitioner's energy with Gaṇapati's vibration.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a concise exposition of Sanskrit grammar (vyākaraṇa), focusing on the pratyāhāra system (abbreviated phonetic groupings) used in Pāṇinian grammar. It lists the pratyāhāra sūtras and explains how they function to derive grammatical rules, aimed at instructing students like Kātyāyana and beginners.

story.title: Exposition of Grammar (Vyākaraṇa)
story.arc: Skanda teaches the essence of grammar, including pratyāhāra sūtras, for the understanding of Kātyāyana and beginners.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic instruction.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical grammatical exposition.

teachings.teaching: The pratyāhāra system: a set of abbreviated phonetic groupings (e.g., a i u ṇa, etc.) used to formulate grammatical rules.
teachings.lens_note: Technical grammatical instruction.

teachings.teaching: The rule for forming pratyāhāras: an initial vowel or consonant followed by a final indicator (it) denotes all intervening phonemes.
teachings.lens_note: Explanation of the pratyāhāra mechanism.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the rules of sandhi (euphonic combination) in Sanskrit grammar, specifically vowel sandhi and consonant sandhi, with examples. It also mentions the pratyāhāra (abbreviated grammatical formulas) and provides various illustrative compounds.

story.title: Rules of Sandhi in Sanskrit Grammar
story.arc: Instruction on euphonic combinations
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical grammatical rules.

teachings.teaching: Vowel sandhi: when vowels combine, the resulting sound is often a single vowel or diphthong, e.g., 'daṇḍāgram' from 'daṇḍa' + 'agram'.
Consonant sandhi: when consonants meet, changes occur such as assimilation or substitution, e.g., 'bhavāṃś caran' from 'bhavān' + 'caran'.
Visarga sandhi: the visarga (ḥ) changes before certain consonants, e.g., 'svambhartā' from 'svaḥ' + 'bhartā'.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 350) is a grammatical treatise on the declension of nouns (subanta) in Sanskrit, following the system of Kātyāyana. It lists various noun stems (prātipadika) and their inflected forms across cases and numbers, focusing on masculine, feminine, and neuter genders. The chapter is a technical manual for correct word formation, with no narrative or mythological content.

story.title: Vibhakti Siddharūpa (Declension of Nouns)
story.arc: Technical exposition on Sanskrit grammar; no narrative arc.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of the Agni Purana's grammatical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical content with no dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: The correct formation of noun declensions is essential for proper speech and ritual accuracy.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, precise speech (vāk) aligns with the subtle energy of mantras, and grammatical purity supports the efficacy of yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Nouns have three genders (masculine, feminine, neuter) and three numbers (singular, dual, plural).
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the triadic nature of reality (triguṇa) in Kriya-Yoga; mastering these distinctions aids in meditation on the three gunas.

teachings.teaching: The declension of words like 'vṛkṣa' (tree) serves as a model for all similar stems.
teachings.lens_note: Analogous to the Kriya-Yoga principle of using a single technique (e.g., kriya proper) as a model for all spiritual practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a grammatical exposition of feminine gender noun forms (strīliṅgaśabdasiddharūpa) in Sanskrit, listing declensions of various feminine nouns such as ramā, jarā, sarvā, buddhī, nadī, kumārī, śrī, strī, dhenu, jambū, varṣābhū, punarbhū, mātṛ, gau, nau, vāc, sraj, vidvas, bhavatī, dīvyantī, bhātī, tudantī, etc.

story.title: Feminine Gender Noun Declensions
story.arc: Grammatical exposition
story.timeline_note: Not applicable; the chapter is a grammatical treatise.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical linguistic text.

teachings.teaching: The chapter provides the declension patterns for various feminine nouns in Sanskrit, illustrating the forms for different cases and numbers.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Napumsakaśabdasiddharūpaṃ', is a grammatical treatise on neuter gender words. It lists various neuter nouns and their forms, likely as part of a larger section on Sanskrit grammar. The chapter is narrated by Skanda.

story.title: Napumsakaśabdasiddharūpaṃ
story.arc: Grammatical exposition on neuter gender words.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; part of a grammatical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical grammar.

teachings.teaching: The chapter provides the correct forms of neuter gender words in Sanskrit grammar.
teachings.lens_note: No specific lens; plain grammatical instruction.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses grammatical rules for neuter gender words (napuṃsakaśabda) and then introduces the topic of kāraka (case relations). It provides examples of various grammatical forms and explains the different types of kartā (agent), karma (object), and karaṇa (instrument).

story.title: Grammatical Exposition on Neuter Gender and Kāraka
story.arc: Teaching
story.timeline_note: Not applicable; this is a didactic chapter.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical grammatical treatise.

teachings.teaching: The agent (kartā) is independent and is of five types; the object (karma) is of seven types.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the classification of kartā and karma reflects the stages of action and awareness in practice. The independent kartā corresponds to the self as the doer, while the various karmas represent objects of attention.

teachings.teaching: The instrument (karaṇa) is twofold: external and internal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret external karaṇa as physical tools and internal as mental faculties, both essential for yogic action.

teachings.teaching: The recipient (sampradāna) is threefold: the one who prompts, the one who gives, and the one who does not reject.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of offering actions to the divine, where the recipient is ultimately the Self.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the grammatical concept of samāsa (compound words), classifying them into various types such as nitya, anitya, tatpuruṣa, karmadhāraya, bahuvrīhi, etc., with examples.

story.title: Discourse on Compound Words
story.arc: Skanda explains the classification of samāsa to an unnamed audience.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Agni Purana's grammatical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical grammatical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Samāsa is divided into six types, further into twenty-eight, and classified as nitya (permanent) or anitya (non-permanent) based on whether the compound is fixed or formed ad hoc.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the distinction between nitya and anitya compounds can be seen as analogous to the difference between fixed spiritual practices (nitya karma) and occasional ones (naimittika karma).

teachings.teaching: Tatpuruṣa compound has eight subtypes based on the case ending of the first member, e.g., prathamātatpuruṣa, dvitīyātatpuruṣa, etc.
teachings.lens_note: The eightfold classification mirrors the eight limbs of yoga (aṣṭāṅga yoga), suggesting that grammatical structure reflects spiritual discipline.

teachings.teaching: Karmadhāraya compound has seven subtypes, including viśeṣaṇapūrvapada, upamānapūrvapada, etc.
teachings.lens_note: The seven subtypes can be correlated with the seven stages of spiritual progress (bhūmikās) in yoga.

teachings.teaching: Bahuvrīhi compound has seven subtypes, e.g., dvipada, ārūḍhabhavana, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Bahuvrīhi compounds, meaning 'possessing much rice,' symbolize abundance and can be linked to the yogic concept of pūrṇatva (fullness).

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the grammatical topic of taddhita (secondary suffixes) in Sanskrit. It lists various suffixes and their meanings, with examples such as 'vatsala', 'picchila', 'dantura', 'madhura', 'keśara', 'rajasvala', 'dhanī', 'hastī', 'payasvī', 'māyāvī', 'vācāla', 'phalin', 'varhiṇ', 'kekī', 'vṛndāraka', 'śītālu', 'himālu', 'vātula', 'vāśiṣṭha', 'kaurava', 'pāñcāla', 'māthura', 'cāndraka', 'kramaka', 'kauśaka', 'praiyaṅgavīnaka', 'maudgīna', 'kaudravīṇa', 'vaideha', 'dākṣi', 'dāśarathi', 'nārāyaṇādika', 'āśvāyana', 'gārgya', 'vātsaka', 'vainateya', 'cāṭakera', 'gaudheraka', 'gaudhāra', 'kṣatriya', 'kulīna', 'kairavya', 'sugandhi', 'nabhastārakita', and others. The chapter is a technical exposition of Sanskrit grammar.

story.title: Taddhita Suffixes
story.arc: Grammatical exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical grammar.

teachings.teaching: The chapter enumerates various taddhita suffixes and their meanings, such as 'vatsala' meaning affectionate, 'picchila' meaning slippery, etc.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, the precise understanding of language and grammar is seen as a tool for mental clarity and discipline, aiding in the purification of the mind for higher spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana (Chapter 356) is a grammatical treatise on the uṇādi suffixes (uṇādayaḥ pratyayāḥ) that are added to verbal roots (dhātu) to form nouns. It lists various words derived through these suffixes, along with their meanings, such as 'uṇi' meaning a craftsman, 'gomāyu' as a jackal, 'kṛkavāku' as a cock, etc. The chapter is technical and focuses on linguistic derivation rather than narrative.

story.title: Uṇādi Suffixes and Their Meanings
story.arc: Grammatical exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a grammatical list.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical list of word derivations.

teachings.teaching: The uṇādi suffixes are added to verbal roots to form nouns, and their meanings are enumerated.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 357) is a technical exposition on Sanskrit grammar, specifically the tiṅ (verb) inflections. It covers the three voices (active, passive, impersonal), tenses and moods (present, imperative, optative, imperfect, aorist, perfect, periphrastic future, simple future, conditional), and the personal endings for the two conjugations (parasmaipada and ātmanepada). It also lists a number of verbal roots (dhātus) with their meanings.

story.title: Exposition of Verb Inflections
story.arc: Kumāra teaches the rules of tiṅ-vibhakti (verb endings) and verbal roots.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic passage.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely grammatical instruction.

teachings.teaching: The tiṅ (verb) endings are used in all three voices: active (kartari), passive (karmaṇi), and impersonal (bhāve).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the three voices correspond to the three modes of action: active (doer-identified), passive (receptive), and impersonal (witnessing). Mastery of these grammatical distinctions aids in discerning the nature of action in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The present tense (laṭ) is used for current action; the optative (liṅ) for injunction or possibility; the imperative (loṭ) for command or blessing; the imperfect (laṅ) for past action not of today; the aorist (luṅ) and perfect (liṭ) for remote past; the periphrastic future (luṭ) for future action not of today; the simple future (lṛṭ) for future; and the conditional (lṛṅ) for hypotheticals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework views tenses as temporal modes of consciousness: present (immediacy), past (memory), future (anticipation). The optative and imperative reflect volitional and directive aspects of the will, crucial for Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on intentional action.

teachings.teaching: The personal endings for parasmaipada (active) are: first person singular -mi, dual -vas, plural -mas; second person -si, -thas, -tha; third person -ti, -tas, -anti. For ātmanepada (middle): first person -e, -vahe, -mahe; second person -se, -āthe, -dhve; third person -te, -āte, -ante.
teachings.lens_note: These endings encode the agent's relationship to the action. In Kriya-Yoga, the choice of ending reflects the yogi's stance: active (parasmaipada) for outward expression, middle (ātmanepada) for self-referential practice, aligning with the principle of 'action for the self'.

teachings.teaching: A list of verbal roots (dhātus) is given, including bhū (to be), pac (to cook), nand (to rejoice), dhvaṃs (to perish), śraṃs (to fall), pad (to go), ad (to eat), śīṅ (to lie), krīḍ (to play), hu (to sacrifice), hā (to leave), dhā (to hold), div (to shine), svap (to sleep), nah (to bind), su (to press), vas (to dwell), tud (to strike), mṛś (to touch), muc (to release), rudh (to obstruct), bhuj (to enjoy), tyaj (to abandon), stan (to thunder), kṛ (to do), etc.
teachings.lens_note: Each root embodies a fundamental action or state. In Kriya-Yoga, meditating on the root meaning (dhātu) of a verb used in practice can deepen the experiential understanding of the action, linking grammar to inner transformation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana is a grammatical treatise on Sanskrit verb forms (tiṅanta), specifically focusing on the root √bhū (to be) and its derivatives, including kṛt suffixes, tense formations, and nominal derivatives. It is a technical text with no narrative or mythological content.

story.title: Grammatical Forms of √bhū
story.arc: Technical exposition of Sanskrit grammar
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; purely didactic.
story.comic_potential: None; highly technical.

teachings.teaching: The root √bhū and its derivatives are used in various grammatical contexts including bhāva (state), karma (object), and kartari (agent).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the focus on verb forms (kriyā) mirrors the importance of action and its transformation. The detailed analysis of √bhū (to be) reflects the yogic principle of 'being' as the foundation of all action.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana is a lexicon of synonyms and classifications for various categories of existence, including celestial beings, natural phenomena, time divisions, virtues, sounds, musical instruments, and dramatic arts. It serves as a reference for names and attributes of deities, demons, and other entities, as well as terms related to cosmology, ethics, and aesthetics.

story.title: Chapter 359
story.arc: This chapter from the Agni Purana is a lexicon of synonyms and classifications for various categories of existence, including celestial beings, natural phenomena, time divisions, virtues, sounds, musical instruments, and dramatic arts. It serves as a reference for names and attributes of deities, demons, and other entities, as well as terms related to cosmology, ethics, and aesthetics.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 360) is a grammatical treatise listing indeclinable words (avyayas) and their meanings, organized by semantic categories. It provides definitions and usage contexts for particles like ā, ku, ca, svasti, svit, sakṛt, etc., as well as interjections and other indeclinables.

story.title: Avyaya-varga: The Section on Indeclinables
story.arc: This chapter is a standalone grammatical exposition within the Agni Purana, not part of a narrative arc.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic section.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical list of grammatical particles.

teachings.teaching: The chapter enumerates indeclinable words (avyayas) and their meanings, such as ā (slight meaning, pervasion, limit), ku (bad, censure), ca (aggregation, collection), etc.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, the precise understanding of language particles is essential for correct mantra recitation and meditation, as each particle carries subtle energetic and cognitive effects that influence the practitioner's state.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists homonyms and synonyms for various words, covering a wide range of meanings from celestial realms to everyday objects, emotions, and actions. It is a lexical compilation without narrative or teaching.

story.title: Nānārthavargāḥ (Chapter on Homonyms)
story.arc: Lexical enumeration
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a list of word meanings.
story.comic_potential: None

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 362) is a lexicon listing synonyms and names for various categories: earth, cities, buildings, trees, plants, herbs, animals, and birds. It is a continuation of the nānārthavarga (section on synonyms) and provides a comprehensive vocabulary for these subjects.

story.title: Nānārthavarga: Bhūmi, Vana, Oṣadhi, Siṃha, etc.
story.arc: Lexicon
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic list.
story.comic_potential: Low

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists various Sanskrit synonyms for human social categories (varnas), family relations, life stages, diseases, and body parts. It is a lexical compilation without narrative.

story.title: Synonyms for Human Categories and Body Parts
story.arc: Lexical list
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline
story.comic_potential: None

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 364) defines terms related to lineage, ritual roles, and spiritual practices. It covers concepts such as family lineage (vaṃśa, gotra), types of priests (ṛtvij, adhvaryu, udgātṛ, hotā), sacrificial implements (caṣāla, yūpa), offerings (āmikṣā, pṛṣadājya, pāyasa), and ascetic types (bhikṣu, parivrāṭ, yati). The chapter also discusses synonyms for worship (pūjā, namasyā, arcā) and the distinction between primary and secondary rituals (mukhya kalpa, anukalpa).

story.title: Definitions of Ritual and Spiritual Terms
story.arc: Expository
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic chapter.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of definitions.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between primary (mukhya) and secondary (anukalpa) rituals, and the importance of proper procedure (vidhi) and discrimination (viveka).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need for disciplined practice (kriya) and discernment in spiritual actions, avoiding mere mechanical repetition.

teachings.teaching: Worship (pūjā) is synonymous with service (varivasyā, śuśrūṣā) and meditation (upāsanam).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views worship as an internal yogic practice, not just external ritual; true pūjā is the offering of one's actions and breath to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Ascetics (yati, yatina) are those who have conquered the senses (nirjitendriyagrāma).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on sense control as a prerequisite for higher meditation and pranayama.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana defines the terminology and hierarchy of the Kshatriya (warrior/ruler), Vaishya (merchant/farmer), and Shudra (servant) classes, including various titles, roles, and objects related to warfare, administration, and daily life. It also covers weights, measures, metals, and professions.

story.title: Kṣatraviṭśūdravargaḥ — The Classes of Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Shudras
story.arc: Encyclopedic listing of terms and definitions related to social classes, warfare, and professions.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic enumeration.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a glossary of terms without dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The chapter provides a detailed classification of social roles, military terms, and objects, emphasizing the importance of precise terminology in dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this classification can be seen as a means to understand the external order (vyavastha) that supports inner discipline (niyama). The precise naming of roles and objects reflects the yogic principle of viveka (discrimination) applied to worldly life.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists synonyms and definitions for various qualities, behaviors, and states of beings, categorized by social classes (kshatriya, vaishya, shudra). It provides a lexicon of terms describing virtues, vices, and characteristics.

story.title: Sāmānyanāmaliṅgāni (Common Names and Characteristics)
story.arc: Lexical exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic list.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a catalog of synonyms.

teachings.teaching: The chapter enumerates synonyms for virtuous and vicious qualities, emphasizing the importance of precise language in describing human traits.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of svādhyāya (self-study) and precise observation of one's own mental states to cultivate sattvic qualities.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the four types of pralaya (dissolution): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛta (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It focuses on the naimittika pralaya at the end of a kalpa, where a severe drought occurs for a hundred years, Vishnu absorbs all waters, seven suns appear and burn the three worlds, then Rudra (from Vishnu's breath) burns everything, and beings move to higher lokas.

story.title: The Four Types of Dissolution
story.arc: Cosmic dissolution and the cycle of creation and destruction
story.timeline_note: Occurs at the end of a kalpa (1000 yuga cycles)
story.comic_potential: Vishnu drinking all waters, seven suns blazing, Rudra burning everything, beings fleeing to higher worlds.

teachings.teaching: There are four types of pralaya: nitya (constant death of beings), naimittika (occasional at kalpa end), prākṛta (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate through knowledge).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the nitya pralaya corresponds to the constant dissolution of ego in meditation; naimittika pralaya is the temporary dissolution of mind in deep samadhi; prākṛta pralaya is the dissolution of the subtle body; ātyantika pralaya is the final liberation.

teachings.teaching: During naimittika pralaya, a severe hundred-year drought occurs, then Vishnu absorbs all waters into the sun's rays, which become seven suns that burn the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: The drought symbolizes the drying up of desires; the seven suns represent the seven senses burning away attachments.

teachings.teaching: Rudra, from Vishnu's breath, burns everything, and beings move to Maharloka and then Janaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Rudra as the fire of self-knowledge burns the ego; the ascent to higher lokas represents the soul's journey to higher states of consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ultimate dissolution (ātyantika laya) and the process of rebirth. It details the types of suffering (ādhyātmika, ādhibhautika, etc.), the journey of the soul after death through the ātivāhika body, the role of Yama and Citragupta, the stages of embryonic development, and the classification of guṇas (sāttvika, rājasa, tāmasa) as they manifest in human nature.

story.title: Ātyantika Laya and the Cycle of Rebirth
story.arc: Cosmic dissolution and individual liberation
story.timeline_note: The chapter discusses the process after death and before rebirth, as well as the ultimate dissolution at the end of a kalpa.
story.comic_potential: The vivid descriptions of the soul's journey, the tortures of hell, and the stages of embryonic development could be depicted in a graphic novel style.

teachings.teaching: Ātyantika laya (ultimate dissolution) is achieved through knowledge (jñāna) and detachment (virāga) from the threefold suffering (ādhyātmika, ādhibhautika, ādhidaivika).
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self through disciplined practice, leading to liberation from the cycle of rebirth.

teachings.teaching: The soul after death assumes an ātivāhika body, which is then led by Yama's messengers to Yama's realm for judgment based on karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the subtle body (sūkṣma śarīra) that carries the karmic impressions, which must be purified through yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: The classification of guṇas (sāttvika, rājasa, tāmasa) determines one's nature and actions.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guṇas are seen as energies that can be transcended through meditation and prāṇāyāma, leading to sattva predominance.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the anatomy and physiology of the human body, including the sense organs, motor organs, the five great elements, the 24 tattvas, the seven or eight āśayas (receptacles), the formation of various organs from the combination of humors (vāta, pitta, kapha) and tissues (rasa, rakta, māṃsa, etc.), and the locations of prāṇa. It also discusses the role of the puruṣa (jīva) and the ultimate goal of knowing the supreme puruṣa.

story.title: Śarīrāvayavāḥ (The Parts of the Body)
story.arc: Exposition of human anatomy and physiology as taught by Agni to a sage (mune).
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; it is a didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical description of the body.

teachings.teaching: The body consists of 24 tattvas (principles) including the five sense organs, five motor organs, five sense objects, five great elements, and mind, with the puruṣa (self) as the 25th.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this enumeration of tattvas corresponds to the microcosmic structure that the yogi must understand and transcend through kriya practices, realizing the puruṣa as distinct from prakṛti.

teachings.teaching: The jīva (individual self) resides in the heart, which is shaped like a lotus and faces downward.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes meditation on the heart lotus (hṛdaya padma) as a key technique for prāṇāyāma and inward focus, leading to the realization of the jīva's true nature.

teachings.teaching: Knowing the supreme puruṣa leads to the supreme state (paramaṃ sthāna).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the goal of Kriya-Yoga: through disciplined practice, the yogi attains direct knowledge of the puruṣa and liberation.

teachings.teaching: The body contains seven or eight āśayas (receptacles) for various humors and fluids.
teachings.lens_note: Understanding these āśayas helps the yogi in practices like cleansing (dhauti) and controlling the bodily elements, as taught in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the process of death, the journey of the soul, and the various hells (narakas) that sinners experience. It details how the vital airs leave the body, the soul's transition through an intermediate body (ātivāhika deha), and the enumeration of 28 crore hells with their names and torments. The chapter concludes by emphasizing that knowledge and yoga, along with austerities, donations, and worship of Vishnu, can destroy the cycle of rebirth.

story.title: Narakanirūpaṇa: Description of Hells
story.arc: The chapter is a didactic exposition by Agni on the process of death and the fate of sinners in various hells, concluding with the means to escape rebirth.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Agni Purana's teachings on karma and afterlife, not tied to a specific narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: The vivid descriptions of hellish torments and the soul's journey could be depicted in a graphic novel style with grotesque imagery.

teachings.teaching: At death, the vital heat (ūṣmā) becomes disturbed by wind, causing the soul to leave through various orifices depending on karma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the pranic disturbances during the withdrawal of consciousness; the yogi learns to control the exit through the crown (mūrdhāna) by mastering prāṇa and apāna.

teachings.teaching: The soul (jīva) leaves the body and assumes an intermediate body (ātivāhika deha) that is taken by Yama's messengers to the hells.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the ātivāhika deha as the subtle body (sūkṣma śarīra) that carries karmic impressions; Kriya-Yoga aims to purify this body through meditation and prāṇāyāma.

teachings.teaching: There are 28 crore hells (naraka-koṭi) located below the earth, with various names and torments for different sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views these hells as symbolic representations of mental states and karmic consequences; the yogi transcends them by realizing the self as distinct from the body and mind.

teachings.teaching: The hells include Raurava, Andhatāmisra, Asipatra, Kālasūtra, etc., where sinners are tortured by Yama's agents.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are seen as projections of the mind's impurities; through disciplined practice, one can overcome the tendencies that lead to such experiences.

teachings.teaching: The cycle of rebirth (saṃsāra) can be destroyed by knowledge and yoga, along with austerities, donations, and worship of Viṣṇu.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga, as a practical path, combines jñāna (knowledge) and dhyāna (meditation) to directly experience the self, thereby cutting the root of saṃsāra.

chapter_summary: Agni Purana Chapter 371, titled 'Yamaniyamāḥ', presents the eight limbs of yoga (aṣṭāṅgayoga) with a focus on the first two: yama (restraints) and niyama (observances). It details the five yamas—ahiṃsā (non-violence), satya (truthfulness), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacarya (celibacy), and aparigraha (non-possessiveness)—and the five niyamas—śauca (purity), santoṣa (contentment), tapas (austerity), svādhyāya (self-study), and īśvarapūjana (worship of God). The chapter elaborates on ahiṃsā as the highest dharma, defines truthfulness, and extensively discusses brahmacarya, including its eight aspects and the dangers of women and intoxicants.

story.title: Yamaniyamāḥ
story.arc: Teaching on the first two limbs of aṣṭāṅgayoga
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely instructional.

teachings.teaching: The eight-limbed yoga (aṣṭāṅgayoga) is taught for liberation from worldly suffering and for illuminating Brahman. Yoga is one-pointedness of mind, cessation of mental modifications, and union of jīva and Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practical path of inner purification and concentration leading to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The five yamas are ahiṃsā, satya, asteya, brahmacarya, and aparigraha. They grant enjoyment and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views yamas as foundational ethical disciplines that purify the mind for deeper yogic practice.

teachings.teaching: The five niyamas are śauca, santoṣa, tapas, svādhyāya, and īśvarapūjana.
teachings.lens_note: Niyamas are observances that cultivate inner strength and devotion, essential for Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Ahiṃsā is non-injury to beings and is the highest dharma. All other dharmas are subsumed under it, like footprints under an elephant's foot.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes ahiṃsā as the root of all virtues, aligning with the non-violent foundation of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Hiṃsā (violence) includes causing distress, pain, injury, bleeding, slander, forbidding what is beneficial, striking vital spots, depriving of happiness, confinement, and killing—ten forms.
teachings.lens_note: This detailed classification helps practitioners avoid subtle forms of violence in thought, word, and deed.

teachings.teaching: Truthfulness (satya) is speech that is entirely beneficial to beings. One should speak what is true and pleasant, not what is true but unpleasant, nor what is pleasant but false. This is the eternal dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that truth must be tempered with compassion, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga communication.

teachings.teaching: Brahmacarya (celibacy) has eight aspects: thinking about, talking about, playing with, gazing at, secret conversation, intention, determination, and actual sexual act. These are the eight limbs of sexual indulgence. Brahmacarya is the root of all practices; otherwise, practice is fruitless.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that brahmacarya is not merely abstinence but control of all senses and mental tendencies, vital for Kriya-Yoga energy conservation.

teachings.teaching: Even great sages and gods like Vasiṣṭha, Moon, Śukra, Bṛhaspati, and Brahmā were deluded by women. There are three types of intoxicants: gauḍī (from molasses), paiṣṭī (from grain), and mādhvī (from honey). The fourth intoxicant is woman, by which the world is deluded. One becomes intoxicated by seeing a woman, just as by drinking liquor. Since women are intoxicating even by sight, one should not look at them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a warning against sensory attachment, advocating for disciplined perception in Kriya-Yoga to maintain mental clarity.

teachings.teaching: Stealing any object, even a small one, by force leads to animal birth, especially if one eats offerings meant for the gods.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the yama of asteya (non-stealing) as essential for karmic purity.

teachings.teaching: One should wear only a loincloth and a rag to cover the body, and use wooden sandals to protect from cold.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as an instruction for minimalism and detachment, supporting the niyama of tapas.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 372) provides detailed instructions on the practices of āsana (posture), prāṇāyāma (breath control), and pratyāhāra (withdrawal of senses) as means to attain mental concentration and spiritual purification. It describes the proper setup for meditation, the technique of prāṇāyāma with its three phases (recaka, pūraka, kumbhaka), the classification of prāṇāyāma by duration, and the benefits of controlling the senses. The chapter also includes analogies such as the body as a chariot and the mind as the charioteer, emphasizing the importance of discipline for self-realization.

story.title: Instructions on Āsana, Prāṇāyāma, and Pratyāhāra
story.arc: The chapter is a didactic exposition by the deity Agni, teaching the practices of yoga for spiritual purification and control of the senses.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the teaching is presented as timeless wisdom.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical manual on yogic practices without narrative or dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: The practice of prāṇāyāma, consisting of recaka (exhalation), pūraka (inhalation), and kumbhaka (retention), is essential for controlling the mind and senses.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, this teaching emphasizes the direct manipulation of prāṇa (life force) to still the mind, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga focus on prāṇāyāma as a primary technique for spiritual advancement.

teachings.teaching: The body is a chariot, the senses are the horses, the mind is the charioteer, and prāṇāyāma is the whip; through knowledge and dispassion, one can control the mind and attain steadiness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this analogy as a call to integrate discriminative knowledge (jñāna) and detachment (vairāgya) with prāṇāyāma practice to achieve mastery over the mind, which is the essence of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Controlling the senses leads to control over everything; the senses themselves are the cause of heaven and hell depending on whether they are restrained or indulged.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasis on pratyāhāra (sense withdrawal) as a crucial step to turn the mind inward, thereby transcending dualities and achieving inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The syllable Oṅkāra encompasses all Vedas and is the ultimate object of meditation; its four mātrās correspond to the states of consciousness and the deities Brahma, Viṣṇu, and Maheśvara.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views Oṅkāra meditation as a means to unify the individual consciousness with the cosmic, a core practice in Kriya-Yoga for realizing the non-dual Brahman.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana defines meditation (dhyāna) as continuous contemplation of Viṣṇu, describes the fourfold aspects of meditation (meditator, meditation, object, purpose), and outlines the practice of inner worship through meditation as superior to external rituals. It details the visualization of a lotus within the body, the syllable Oṃ, and the form of Viṣṇu, emphasizing that meditation leads to liberation and the eight siddhis.

story.title: The Teaching on Meditation (Dhyāna)
story.arc: Instruction on yoga and meditation
story.timeline_note: Part of the Agni Purana's exposition on yoga practices.
story.comic_potential: A serene, contemplative scene of a meditator visualizing a lotus and the divine form.

teachings.teaching: Meditation is the continuous contemplation of Viṣṇu with an undistracted mind.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of constant remembrance (smaraṇa) as a means to still the mind and realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: Meditation involves four aspects: meditator, meditation, object, and purpose.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that understanding these four is crucial for systematic practice, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga stages of discernment.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of meditation is liberation and the eight siddhis (aṇimā, etc.).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views the siddhis as natural byproducts of deep meditation, not goals; liberation is the ultimate aim.

teachings.teaching: Meditation should be practiced constantly, in all states (walking, standing, sleeping, waking).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on integrating awareness into daily activities, not just seated practice.

teachings.teaching: Inner meditation (dhyānayajña) is superior to external rituals because it is free from harm and purifies the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that internal worship through meditation is the essence of Kriya-Yoga, transcending outer forms.

teachings.teaching: Visualize a lotus in the heart with eight petals, representing the eight siddhis, and within it the syllable Oṃ as the form of Īśvara.
teachings.lens_note: This visualization is a key Kriya-Yoga technique for focusing the mind and awakening subtle energies.

teachings.teaching: Meditate on Viṣṇu with four or eight arms, holding conch, discus, mace, lotus, etc., adorned with ornaments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets these forms as symbolic representations of divine attributes, aiding concentration.

teachings.teaching: Alternate between meditation and mantra repetition (japa); both lead to Viṣṇu's grace.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga combines japa and dhyāna as complementary practices to stabilize the mind.

teachings.teaching: Japa is superior to all other sacrifices; it protects from diseases, afflictions, and planetary influences.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the power of mantra repetition as a direct means to purify the mind and invoke divine protection.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 374) explains the practice of dhāraṇā (concentration) as a precursor to dhyāna (meditation) and samādhi (absorption). It describes various types of dhāraṇā—āgneyī (fiery), vāruṇī (watery), aiśānī (supreme), and amṛtātmikā (nectar-like)—and their effects on the body and mind. The text emphasizes fixing the mind on a chosen object (lakṣya) and gradually withdrawing from external distractions, leading to spiritual liberation.

story.title: Dhāraṇā: The Practice of Concentration
story.arc: Instructional discourse by Agni on yogic techniques
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of Agni Purana's teachings on yoga.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily technical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Dhāraṇā is the steady fixation of the mind on a chosen object, preventing it from wavering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the initial stage of pratyāhāra (sense withdrawal) where the mind is trained to hold a single point, preparing for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: Twelve dhāraṇās constitute one dhyāna, and twelve dhyānas constitute one samādhi.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the graduated nature of practice: dhāraṇā builds concentration, dhyāna extends it into flow, and samādhi is absorption. This verse provides a quantitative measure for progress.

teachings.teaching: One who practices dhāraṇā and dies attains liberation, lifting twenty-one generations of family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the power of focused practice to purify karmic lineage, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga goal of ancestral liberation.

teachings.teaching: For ailments in specific body parts, the yogi should concentrate on that part with tattva-dhāraṇā (concentration on the element).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a therapeutic application of dhāraṇā, where pranic energy is directed to heal, akin to Kriya-Yoga's use of pranayama for health.

teachings.teaching: The dhāraṇās are of four types: āgneyī (fiery), vāruṇī (watery), aiśānī (supreme), and amṛtātmikā (nectar-like).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma correlates these with the elements and chakras: āgneyī for manipura, vāruṇī for svadhisthana, aiśānī for ajna, and amṛtātmikā for sahasrara, guiding the practitioner through the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: Visualize a divine, radiant spike piercing through the nadis from the big toe to the crown, surrounded by a sphere of light.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma describes this as a kriya technique to awaken the sushumna, using the 'spike' as a metaphor for pranic force rising through the central channel.

teachings.teaching: The body becomes ash-like and then one withdraws the senses; cold, phlegm, and sins are destroyed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets 'ash-like' as the state of vayustambha (pranic suspension), where the body is inert yet conscious, leading to purification.

teachings.teaching: Meditate on the head, throat, and heart with uninterrupted awareness, merging with the self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a sequential focus on ajna, vishuddhi, and anahata chakras, culminating in self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Visualize cool, sparkling streams filling the universe, originating from the moon.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies this as the vāruṇī dhāraṇā, a cooling practice to balance the fiery elements, often used in pranayama for calming the mind.

teachings.teaching: From the brahmarandhra, the practitioner enters the sushumna and floods the body with nectar-like coolness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma describes this as the amṛtātmikā dhāraṇā, where the yogi experiences the flow of amrita (nectar) from the crown, a key Kriya-Yoga experience.

teachings.teaching: The vāruṇī dhāraṇā is used to alleviate hunger, thirst, and heat.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes this as a practical application for bodily discomfort, using mental visualization to regulate the elements.

teachings.teaching: In aiśānī dhāraṇā, the prāṇa and apāna merge in the cosmic lotus in the sky, and one visualizes Viṣṇu's grace until thoughts cease.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the culmination of pranayama where prana and apana unite at the ajna chakra, leading to samadhi.

teachings.teaching: One should chant the mahābhāva (great feeling) and realize the all-pervading Lord, who is like a half-moon, supremely peaceful, without appearance or stain.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as the final stage of meditation, where the practitioner merges with the formless Brahman, beyond all dualities.

teachings.teaching: The world appears real only until one sees one's own true form as taught by the guru.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the necessity of guru's grace to realize the self, after which the world is seen as illusory.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 375) defines samādhi as a state of deep meditation where the mind becomes still like a lamp in a windless place, merging with the divine. It describes the characteristics of a yogi in samādhi, the obstacles and attainments (siddhis) that arise, and the process of liberation through knowledge. The chapter also discusses the subtle anatomy of light rays (raśmis) within the heart, leading to higher realms.

story.title: The Nature of Samādhi
story.arc: Instruction on meditation and liberation
story.timeline_note: Timeless teaching; no narrative sequence.
story.comic_potential: Low; abstract philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Samādhi is a state where the mind becomes still like a lamp in a windless place, merging with the divine consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the culmination of pratyahara and dharana, where the mind is absorbed in the inner light.

teachings.teaching: A yogi in samādhi is oblivious to sensory inputs, like a log of wood, and is merged with Īśvara.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the state of nirbija samadhi where all vrittis cease, as emphasized in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Various siddhis (divine powers) arise, but the yogi should disregard them to attain Viṣṇu's grace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma warns against attachment to siddhis as distractions from the ultimate goal of kaivalya.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is bound by karma and delusion but liberated through knowledge; the yogi becomes free from disease.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on jnana and kriya to purify karma.

teachings.teaching: Within the heart, there are rays of light; one ray pierces the sun's orb and leads to the supreme goal beyond Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the sushumna nadi and the journey through chakras to the sahasrara.

teachings.teaching: The field (kṣetra) and knower of the field (kṣetrajña) are distinct; Īśvara pervades all as both sat and asat.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga distinction between prakriti and purusha.

chapter_summary: Agni reveals the knowledge of Brahman (brahmajñāna) for liberation from the ignorance of samsara. The self (ātman) is not the body, senses, mind, or prāṇa, but the witness and experiencer distinct from them. The creation proceeds from Brahman to ether, air, fire, water, earth, and the subtle and gross bodies. The meditator should contemplate the dissolution of the gross body into Brahman.

story.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
story.arc: Agni imparts the teaching of self-realization through discrimination and meditation on the creation and dissolution of the universe.
story.timeline_note: This chapter is a discourse by Agni, likely part of a larger dialogue in the Agni Purana.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a dense philosophical exposition with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is not the body, senses, mind, or prāṇa, but the witness and experiencer distinct from them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the initial discrimination (viveka) where the practitioner separates the self from the non-self, a prerequisite for meditation.

teachings.teaching: The creation proceeds from Brahman to ether, air, fire, water, earth, and the subtle and gross bodies.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this sequence is meditated upon as the process of involution (laya) during pratyahara, leading to absorption in Brahman.

teachings.teaching: The meditator should contemplate the dissolution of the gross body into Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: This is the practice of laya yoga, where the yogi visualizes the reversal of creation, culminating in samadhi.

chapter_summary: Agni reveals the nature of the supreme Brahman as the supreme light, negating all attributes and identifications with the gross, subtle, and causal bodies, states of consciousness, and cosmic principles.

story.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
story.arc: Agni teaches the nature of Brahman as the supreme light, devoid of all attributes and identifications.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; purely didactic.
story.comic_potential: Low; abstract philosophical negation.

teachings.teaching: Brahman is the supreme light, free from all elements, senses, mind, intellect, ego, prāṇas, states of consciousness, and cosmic forms.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this negation corresponds to the progressive withdrawal of identification from the gross (sthūla), subtle (sūkṣma), and causal (kāraṇa) bodies, leading to the realization of the pure Self beyond all modifications.

chapter_summary: Agni reveals the five paths to liberation: through sacrifices one attains the realm of Virāj, through austerity a higher realm, through renunciation of actions one merges with Prakṛti, through knowledge one attains Kaivalya. He explains detachment, renunciation, and the nature of knowledge as discrimination between sentient and insentient. The supreme Self, called Viṣṇu, is the support of all. He is worshipped through pravṛtti (active) and nivṛtti (renunciate) paths. The chapter also discusses the two kinds of Brahman: śabdabrahman (scriptural) and parabrahman (transcendental), and the meaning of the syllable 'bhaga' as denoting opulence.

story.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
story.arc: Agni teaches the paths to liberation and the nature of Brahman
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; abstract philosophical teaching.
